segments.p2-4-g7: Что is the interrogative pronoun 'what'. The particle же adds emphasis or impatience (roughly 'on earth'/'then') without changing grammar. Это is the demonstrative pronoun 'this/it' (neuter), and значило is the past-tense, neuter form of значить 'to mean/indicate', agreeing with the neuter pronoun это; the word order is the normal Russian question order (interrogative + subject + verb).
segments.p2-4-g8: После governs the genitive: 'после многих расспросов' = 'after many questionings'. Добился (perfective, past, masculine singular) with the reflexive ending -ся here means 'managed to obtain' or 'succeeded in getting', and it commonly takes a genitive object introduced by от (от слуг = 'from the servants'). Наконец is an adverb 'finally' modifying добился.
segments.p2-4-g9: Что here is the conjunction 'that' introducing reported content. Обед is the noun 'dinner' (subject of the subordinate clause). Заказан is the short passive participle / passive predicate 'is/has been ordered' (agreeing with обед). К шести часам expresses time with к + dative ('for/by six o'clock' / 'at six o'clock').
segments.p2-4-g10: А introduces a contrast ('but/and' here functioning like 'not ... but ...'); не negates the following time phrase; к пяти is again k + dative indicating the (contrasted) time 'at five o'clock'. The clause contrasts the actual time (six) with the undesired earlier time (five).
segments.p2-4-g11: Это is the demonstrative 'that/this' serving as the direct object of подтвердили or as the thing being confirmed (accusative identical to nominative). Подтвердили is past tense plural of подтвердить 'confirmed' with an implied plural subject ('they' / people there). И here means 'also', and в буфете is the prepositional phrase 'at the buffet' (в + prepositional) indicating location.
segments.p2-4-g12: Даже is an adverb meaning 'even' intensifying the statement. Стыдно is an impersonal predicative meaning 'it is shameful/one feels ashamed'; стало is past neuter of стать 'became', forming the impersonal construction 'стало стыдно' = 'it became shameful/I felt ashamed'. Расспрашивать is the infinitive 'to ask questions/to inquire', acting as the complement indicating what it became shameful to do.
segments.p2-4-g1: Subject + adverbial time phrase + past verb: 'Я' is the subject, followed by the temporal modifier 'еще накануне' (literally 'already/on the day before') and the past-tense verb 'знал' (I knew). 'Накануне' is an adverb meaning 'on the eve' or 'the day before' and here combines with 'еще' (= 'already/yet') to indicate he already knew before the event.
segments.p2-4-g2: 'что' introduces a subordinate clause (object clause of 'знал'). 'Приеду' is the first-person singular future tense of приехать ('I will arrive/come'), and 'первый' is an ordinal predicate meaning 'the first (one)'. Together: 'that I would arrive first.'
segments.p2-4-g3: 'Но' is a contrastive conjunction ('but'). 'Уж' is an emphatic particle here (roughly 'indeed'/'in any case'). The impersonal phrase 'дело было' functions like 'the point/thing was', and the prepositional phrase 'не в первенстве' means 'not in primacy/being first' — together: 'But the point was not in being first.'
segments.p2-4-g4: 'Их' here is a colloquial use of the pronoun meaning 'them/they' used as the subject (common in spoken or narrative Russian). 'Не только' begins a 'not only ... but ...' contrast, and 'никого не было' is an impersonal construction meaning 'there was nobody' or 'no one was there.' Together the clause conveys that not merely some particular person but absolutely nobody of them was present.
segments.p2-4-g5: 'Но' connects the contrasting second half. 'Я' is the subject; 'даже' is an adverb of emphasis ('even'); 'едва' means 'barely/hardly' and modifies the past verb 'отыскал' ('found/located'). 'Нашу комнату' is the direct object in the accusative. Word order is standard S-V-O: 'I even barely found our room.'
segments.p2-4-g6: 'На столе' is a locative prepositional phrase ('on the table'). The impersonal/copular 'было' plus the short passive participle 'накрыто' forms a predicate meaning 'was set/covered.' The adverbial cluster 'еще не совсем' modifies the predicate and means 'not yet completely' (so the whole says the table was not yet fully set). Note that 'накрыто' is the neuter short form used in such impersonal descriptions.
segments.p2-4-g24: "К шести часам" uses the preposition к + dative to mark a deadline or point in time ("by six o'clock"). The numeral phrase appears in the dative plural (шести часам) to express that temporal limit.
segments.p2-4-g25: "кроме" governs the genitive and means "aside from / besides." "горевших" is a past (verbal) participle of гореть agreeing with ламп in gender/number/case and describes lamps that were burning; together, кроме горевших ламп = "apart from/aside from the lamps that were burning."
segments.p2-4-g26: This is a passive past construction: внесены (past passive participle) + были (past auxiliary) = "had been brought" (were brought). Word order is inverted for emphasis: the directional phrase в комнату (accusative) marks motion "into the room," свечи is the nominative plural subject ("candles").
segments.p2-4-g27: Слуга (subject) plus the negated past verb не подумал (perfective past) means "the servant did not think" or "did not think to (do something)." Here подумал is used with an infinitive complement (внести) to indicate he failed to consider performing that action.
segments.p2-4-g28: однако is a parenthetical conjunction meaning "however / nevertheless." The enclitic particle ж adds emphasis or insistence; punctuation shows this phrase as a brief afterthought or corrective remark.
segments.p2-4-g29: внести is the infinitive complement of подумал ("to bring in"). их is the accusative plural pronoun "them" (referring to the candles). тотчас же is an adverbial phrase meaning "immediately / at once," with же reinforcing immediacy.
segments.p2-4-g30: как here is a temporal conjunction meaning "when / as soon as." я приехал is a simple past first-person clause ("I arrived") giving the time when the action should have happened.
segments.p2-4-g31: В соседней комнате is a locative prepositional phrase ('in the neighboring room') with в + prepositional (комнате). Обедали is past-tense, plural, imperfective, used impersonally/with an omitted subject to mean 'were dining' or 'had been dining' — word order places the place first, then the verb.
segments.p2-4-g32: на разных столах is another locative phrase ('at different/separate tables') using на + prepositional plural (столах); it's an adverbial modifier placed between clauses to specify location of the dining.
segments.p2-4-g33: два какие-то мрачных посетителя contains the numeral два followed by the indefinite particle phrase какие-то plus adjective and noun. Note the grammar quirk: with numerals 2–4 the noun takes the genitive singular (посетителя) while the adjective appears in genitive plural (мрачных); какие-то here means 'some/some kind of' and makes the whole description indefinite.
segments.p2-4-g34: сердитые с виду и молчавшие is a coordinate adjectival/participle phrase describing the visitors. Сердитые с виду = 'angry / appearing angry' (с + виду is a fixed expression meaning 'in appearance'), and молчавшие is a past-active participle ('silent' / 'having been silent'). Both agree in number with the implied plural referent.
segments.p2-4-g35: В одной из дальних комнат is a complex locative phrase: в + ordinal/indefinite one (одной) + из + genitive plural (комнат) = 'in one of the far rooms'. Было очень шумно is an impersonal construction (neuter predicate шумно with past был) meaning 'it was very noisy.'
segments.p2-4-g36: даже кричали is a short clause: даже = 'even' (emphatic) and кричали is past plural imperfective 'they were shouting' or 'people shouted', with the subject omitted and understood from context.
segments.p2-4-g37: слышен был хохот целой ватаги людей is an inversion where слышен ('audible') is a predicative adjective followed by был, giving 'could be heard.' Хохот is the subject in the nominative; целой ватаги людей is a genitive phrase 'of a whole rabble/crowd of people' specifying whose laughter it was.
segments.p2-4-g38: слышались какие-то скверные французские взвизги uses the plural verb слышались ('were heard') agreeing with взвизги (plural). Какие-то is the indefinite particle 'some', and the adjectives скверные французские modify взвизги: 'some nasty French shrieks/squeals.'
segments.p2-4-g39: обед был с дамами is a simple copular clause: обед ('the meal') был ('was') + с + instrumental дамами meaning 'with ladies' — i.e. the meal included/was attended by ladies.
segments.p2-4-g13: Было is an impersonal past-tense form of быть used to state the time (’it was’). ещё только is a double adverbial emphasis meaning ‘still/only’ or ‘just’; двадцать пять минут is the numeral + noun phrase (минут in genitive plural after numerals) meaning ‘twenty-five minutes’; шестого is the genitive of the ordinal шестой and here marks the target hour (’to six’ — literally ‘of the sixth’), a common Russian way to say ‘minutes to six.’
segments.p2-4-g14: Если introduces a conditional clause (‘if’). они is the subject (’they’). переменили is the past tense, perfective verb (переменить) meaning ‘changed’ — a completed action. час is the direct object (’the hour’ / ‘the time of the appointment’). The clause gives the hypothesis that they altered the appointed hour.
segments.p2-4-g15: то is the correlative particle linking this clause to the preceding если (’then’). во всяком случае is an idiom meaning ‘in any case.’ должны были is a past-tense periphrasis expressing obligation that should have been fulfilled (’ought to have’ / ‘should have’); же is an emphatic particle reinforcing the obligation. известить is the infinitive ‘to inform/notify’ and completes the modal construction.
segments.p2-4-g16: на то here means ‘for that’ or ‘for this purpose’ (она предназначена на то — ‘it is for that’). городская почта is a topicalized nominative phrase ‘the city post (office)’ — an elliptical statement meaning ‘that is what the city post is for.’ The order highlights the purpose before naming the institution.
segments.p2-4-g17: а introduces a contrast (‘and/but’). не подвергать is the negative infinitive ‘not to subject/expose (to).’ меня is the accusative object ‘me.’ позору is the dative (or dative-like government) after подвергать — ‘to expose to shame/disgrace.’ перед собой means ‘in front of themselves’ (перед + reflexive себя in instrumental), and the final и is a colloquial/emphatic particle (‘even/too’). The clause objects to exposing the speaker to shame, even before the others themselves.
segments.p2-4-g20: и is a simple connective ‘and.’ хоть here functions as a concessive/limiting particle meaning ‘even’ or ‘at least’ (хоть перед слугами = ‘even in front of the servants’). перед takes the instrumental to mean ‘in the presence of / before,’ and слугами is the instrumental plural of слуга (‘servants’).
segments.p2-4-g21: Я is the subject pronoun ‘I.’ сел is the past masculine singular of сесть, a simple perfective past meaning ‘sat down’ or ‘took a seat.’ The short clause reports the speaker’s action in the past.
segments.p2-4-g22: слуга is the subject ‘the servant.’ стал is the past form of стать used here as an auxiliary meaning ‘began to’ when followed by an infinitive. накрывать is the imperfective infinitive ‘to cover,’ here colloquially ‘to set the table’ (накрывать на стол). Together they mean ‘the servant began to set the table.’
segments.p2-4-g23: при нем is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘in his presence’ or ‘with him there’ (при + pronoun in the appropriate form). стало is impersonal past of стать meaning ‘it became.’ как-то is an adverbial compound meaning ‘somehow’ or ‘for some reason.’ ещё here means ‘even/more’ (it strengthens the comparative), and обиднее is the comparative form of обидный (‘more offensive/hurtful’). The clause says that in the servant’s presence the situation felt somehow still more humiliating.
segments.p2-4-g104: И is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking this action to what came before; он is the 3rd-person masculine singular pronoun 'he' (the subject). Небрежно is an adverb modifying the verb, 'carelessly.' Повернулся is the perfective past tense reflexive verb 'turned (around)' (masculine singular), and положить is an infinitive expressing purpose ('to put'), so the construction повернулся положить means 'he turned in order to put.' На + окно (accusative) indicates placement 'onto/on the window,' and шляпу is the direct object (accusative) of положить, 'his hat.'
segments.p2-4-g105: Давно is an adverb here meaning 'for a long time' (used in questions to ask how long). Ждете is the 2nd-person plural (or polite singular) present tense of ждать, 'you are waiting'/'have you been waiting'. The sentence is a direct question addressed to the visitor.
segments.p2-4-g106: Спросил is the perfective past tense, masculine singular, 'asked' and is used here to report speech; the verb comes before the name for narrative effect. Трудолюбов is a proper name in the nominative, the speaker who asked the question.
segments.p2-4-g107: Я is the first-person pronoun 'I' (subject). Приехал is the perfective past tense masculine singular of приехать, 'arrived' (by vehicle). Ровно is an adverb 'exactly,' and в пять часов is a time expression 'at five o'clock' (в + time with часов in the genitive plural).
segments.p2-4-g108: Как introduces the subordinate clause 'as' (manner), мне is the dative 'to me' indicating the beneficiary/target of the arrangement, вчера is 'yesterday,' and назначили is the past tense plural 'they arranged/appointed/scheduled' — here meaning 'had been arranged for me.' The clause is effectively 'as (they) arranged for me yesterday.'
segments.p2-4-g109: Отвечал is the imperfective past tense 'answered' (masculine), used to report the speaker's reply; the order отвечал я places mild emphasis on 'I answered.' Громко is the adverb 'loudly,' и = 'and,' and с раздражением is a prepositional phrase ('with irritation') using the instrumental case (с + instrumental).
segments.p2-4-g110: Обещавшим is a past active participle in the instrumental singular agreeing with раздражением (i.e. 'with an irritation that promised'); here it functions as a postpositive attribute meaning 'which promised.' Близкий is an adjective 'near' (physical/temporal), and взрыв is the object of обещавшим — literally 'explosion' but used here as 'outburst' (an emotional eruption).
segments.p2-4-g96: Вы is the 2nd-person pronoun (plural or polite singular) used as the subject. Нас is the accusative form of мы here marking the people of whom the subject is afraid/shy; colloquially it functions like an English object ("of us"). Дичитесь is the present indicative, 2nd-person plural/formal, of the reflexive verb дичиться; the reflexive -ся gives the verb an intransitive or passive/experiential sense 'be shy/hold oneself like a stranger' (here: 'shy away from').
segments.p2-4-g97: Напрасно is an adverb functioning as a one-word sentence/interjection meaning 'in vain' or 'needlessly'; it negates the relevance of the previous statement rather than modifying a specific verb here.
segments.p2-4-g98: Мы is the subject pronoun 'we'. Не is the sentential negation placed before the degree adverb так and the predicate; не так ... (= 'not so/that') is a common pattern to deny degree. Страшны is the short-form plural adjective serving as the predicative ('are frightening/terrible'); short-form adjectives form the verbal-predicate in present state.
segments.p2-4-g99: Как introduces the comparative clause 'as/like'. Вам is the dative pronoun marking the experiencer/target of perception. Кажется is the impersonal/experiential 3rd-person form of казаться meaning 'it seems' — with вам it yields 'it seems to you'. Together: 'as it seems to you.'
segments.p2-4-g100: Ну is an interjection meaning 'well' or 'now'; the following -с is an archaic/enclitic particle fused to form ну‑с (common in 19th-century colloquial speech) giving a sense like 'well then' or urging continuation. The 'с' here is not the preposition 'с' and functions as a particle.
segments.p2-4-g101: Во всяком случае is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning 'in any case' (во is the form of в used before consonant clusters, всяком is the prepositional singular of всякий 'any/each', and случае is 'case/instance' in the prepositional). Рад is the short-form masculine adjective 'glad' used predicatively. The final hyphenated pieces (во‑зоб‑но‑вить) represent the infinitive возобновить 'to resume/renew', split by the printer/speaker to show stuttering; grammatically this is an infinitive complement to рад ('glad to resume').
segments.p2-4-g111: 'Разве' is a particle adding surprise or asking for confirmation; here it frames a negative question ('Didn't you...'). 'ты' is the nominative singular 'you' (subject). 'не' negates the verb. 'Дал' is the past masculine singular of дать and with the infinitive 'знать' forms the idiom 'дать знать' = 'to let (someone) know'; the subject 'ты' is the agent. 'Ему' is the dative object 'to him'.
segments.p2-4-g112: 'Что' here is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the content of what should have been told. 'Переменили' is the past plural of 'переменить' (perfective) 'changed' — plural because the implied subject is 'they' (people who changed the schedule). 'Часы' here means 'the time' / 'hours' (the appointment time) and stands in the accusative as the thing that was changed.
segments.p2-4-g113: The past reflexive verb 'оборотился' (masculine singular) means 'turned (oneself) toward/around'; it's placed before the name for stylistic narration. 'Трудолюбов' is the surname in nominative (the subject). 'К' + dative 'Симонову' marks direction 'to/toward Simonov.'
segments.p2-4-g114: A terse negative answer: 'Не' negates the past verb 'дал' (masculine singular). The subject 'я' is omitted and understood from context; the past masculine form shows the speaker is male or the reported actor is male.
segments.p2-4-g115: 'Забыл' is the past masculine singular of 'забыть' ('to forget') used as a single-word reply; the subject ('I' or 'he') is omitted but indicated by the masculine past form.
segments.p2-4-g116: 'Отвечал' is the past tense (imperfective) of 'отвечать' meaning 'answered' in narrative recounting; 'тот' is a demonstrative pronoun 'that one / the one' pointing back to the person who spoke (i.e., 'he').
segments.p2-4-g117: 'Но' introduces contrast ('but'). 'Без' governs the genitive. 'Всякого' is a genitive form of 'всякий' used here in the sense 'any' (i.e., 'without any'). 'Раскаяния' is the genitive of 'раскаяние' meaning 'remorse/repentance'. The conjunction 'и' links this phrase to the following participial clause.
segments.p2-4-g118: 'Даже' is an adverb 'even' for emphasis. 'Не' negates the gerund 'извинившись' (past adverbial participle of 'извиниться'), so the phrase means 'without having apologized.' 'Передо' is a colloquial/allomorph of 'перед' meaning 'before/in front of' and governs the instrumental 'мной' ('me'), so 'передо мной' = 'to me / in my presence.' The gerund indicates an action contemporaneous with the main verb.
segments.p2-4-g119: 'Пошел' is the past masculine singular of 'пойти' ('went' / 'set off' / 'proceeded'), here narrating that he then did something. 'Распоряжаться' is an infinitive meaning 'to manage/arrange/see to' and functions as the complement of 'пошел' (he went to arrange). 'Закуской' is the instrumental singular of 'закуска' and is used after 'распоряжаться' to indicate the thing he was arranging ('the snacks/refreshments').
segments.p2-4-g133: 'Это' is the demonstrative/dummy pronoun used like English 'it' or 'this' (here functioning as a dummy subject, 'it's'); 'вовсе' is an adverb that strengthens the negation ('at all'); 'не' is the sentential negation; 'смешно' is the short neuter adjective used predicatively meaning 'funny'. The word order is standard for a blunt exclamation: pronoun + adverb + negation + predicative adjective.
segments.p2-4-g134: The verb 'закричал' is past masculine singular (perfective), 'shouted/cried out'. The following 'я' is the subject placed after the verb (inversion common in Russian narration), and 'Ферфичкину' is in the dative case (ending -у) marking the indirect object/recipient: 'I shouted to Ferfichkin.'
segments.p2-4-g135: 'раздражаясь' is a деепричастие (adverbial participle/gerund) meaning 'becoming/getting irritated' and shows a simultaneous or attendant action. The phrase 'все более и более' is a fixed intensifying construction meaning 'more and more' (literally 'all more and more'), with repetition of 'более' for emphasis.
segments.p2-4-g136: 'виноваты' is the short plural predicate form of the adjective 'виноватый', used here as a predicative meaning 'are guilty' or 'to blame'; 'другие' is nominative plural 'the others/other people', so together 'the others are to blame.'
segments.p2-4-g137: 'а' is the contrastive conjunction 'but/and' used to set up opposition; 'не' negates the pronoun 'я' ('I'), so the short clause contrasts the guilty others with 'not I' — literally 'but not I.'
segments.p2-4-g138: 'Мне' is dative ('to me'), indicating the person affected. 'Пренебрегли' is past plural of 'пренебречь' meaning 'they neglected/failed to do (something)' and here takes an infinitival complement; 'дать знать' is an infinitive phrase meaning 'to let (someone) know' or 'to inform.' The structure is: (they) neglected to let me know.
segments.p2-4-g139: The repetition of 'Это' separated by dashes is a stammering or emphatic device; each 'это' is the demonstrative/dummy pronoun used as a filler or to point/emphasize ('this' / 'it'), without additional grammatical modification.
segments.p2-4-g40: "Одним словом," is a fixed summarizing phrase meaning "in a word" or "to sum up." Grammatically it is an instrumental singular phrase (одним + словом) used adverbially to mark a brief summary.
segments.p2-4-g41: This is an impersonal past construction: neuter past "было" + predicative "тошно" gives "it was nauseating / unpleasant." "очень" simply intensifies the predicative.
segments.p2-4-g42: Adverb "Редко" is fronted for emphasis ('seldom'); past imperfective verb "проводил" with accusative "минуту" means "to spend/pass (a minute/moment)." Comparative "более скверную минуту" = "a fouler/worse minute."
segments.p2-4-g43: "так что" introduces the result clause ('so that'); "когда они" begins a subordinate temporal clause ('when they...'). Word order is straightforward: conjunction + temporal word + pronoun.
segments.p2-4-g44: "ровно в шесть часов" is a precise time expression: "ровно" (exactly) modifies the prepositional phrase "в шесть часов" ('at six o'clock'), where "в" marks a point in time.
segments.p2-4-g45: "явились" is the perfective past of "явиться" meaning "appeared/arrived"; "все разом" ('all together / all at once') modifies the verb to show simultaneity.
segments.p2-4-g46: Simple nominative pronoun "я" standing alone with a comma for emphasis: subject of the following clauses.
segments.p2-4-g47: "на первый миг" is a temporal accusative phrase meaning "for the first instant/for a moment" ("на" + accusative expresses duration or point in time here).
segments.p2-4-g48: "обрадовался им" is perfective reflexive-type past (обрадоваться) meaning "rejoiced at them"; "как каким-то освободителям" is a simile ('as if to some liberators') where "каким-то" = "some/sort of"; "и чуть не забыл" = "and almost forgot" ("чуть не" = almost + past verb).
segments.p2-4-g49: "что обязан смотреть обиженным" is a subordinate nominal clause introduced by "что" ('that'); "обязан" is the short adjective/participle meaning "obliged/supposed to," and "смотреть обиженным" uses "смотреть" in the sense "to appear / to look" (not literal seeing) with the predicate adjective "обиженным" ('offended').
segments.p2-4-g50: Зверков вошел впереди всех is a main clause: proper name Зверков (subject) + вошел, past singular masculine 'entered', and впереди всех is an adverbial phrase ('in front of everyone' / 'ahead of all') with всех in the genitive to indicate 'of all.'
segments.p2-4-g51: видимо предводительствуя is composed of the parenthetical adverb видимо ('apparently') and the present adverbial participle (деепричастие) предводительствуя from предводительствовать, meaning 'acting as leader' or 'leading' — it describes a simultaneous attendant action to вошел.
segments.p2-4-g52: 'И он и все они' is a correlative 'и...и' construction meaning 'both...and'; the plural past verb 'смеялись' agrees with the combined plural subject. The sentence begins with this contrasted clause followed by a semicolon.
segments.p2-4-g53: The conjunction 'но' introduces a contrast with the previous clause ('but'). It's a straightforward adversative linker.
segments.p2-4-g54: 'Увидя' is a perfective adverbial participle (деепричастие) meaning 'on/when seeing' or 'having seen' and sets the temporal/causal background for the main clause; 'меня' is the accusative object 'me'.
segments.p2-4-g55: A main-clause past reflexive verb: 'приосанился' (perfective past) means 'straightened up' / 'assumed a proud bearing' — a sudden change of posture. 'Зверков' is the character's name (not a common noun here).
segments.p2-4-g56: 'Подошел' is the perfective past of 'подойти' — 'approached' or 'came up'; 'неторопливо' is an adverb modifying the manner ('unhurriedly').
segments.p2-4-g57: 'Перегибаясь' is an imperfective adverbial participle ('bending over'), describing concurrent manner; 'в' is the spatial preposition 'in/at'; 'талье' (tалье) means 'waist' (here locative notion: 'at the waist').
segments.p2-4-g58: 'Точно' here functions as 'as if' (not literal 'exactly'), and 'кокетничая' is another adverbial participle ('flirting' / 'acting coquettishly'), together giving a simile of his manner.
segments.p2-4-g59: 'И' links actions; 'подал' is past of 'подать' meaning 'offered/held out' (transitive); 'мне' is the indirect object 'to me'; 'руку' is the direct object in accusative 'his hand'.
segments.p2-4-g60: 'Ласково' is an adverb describing the manner of offering the hand: 'affectionately' or 'gently'.
segments.p2-4-g61: The short phrase 'но не очень' qualifies the previous adverb: 'but not very (much)'. 'Не' negates 'очень' (very), so 'not very'.
segments.p2-4-g62: 'С' governs an implied noun in the instrumental; 'какой-то' (split here into 'какой' + 'то') is an indefinite particle combination meaning 'some kind of'; 'осторожной' is the adjective in the instrumental case agreeing with the later noun ('вежливостию'), so the whole chunk means 'with a certain cautious...'.
segments.p2-4-g63: 'Чуть' with 'не' forms the idiom 'чуть не' = 'almost'; 'генеральской' is an adjective (instrumental, agreeing with the noun) meaning 'general's'; 'вежливостию' is an older/archaisizing instrumental form of 'вежливость' meaning 'politeness / politeness manifested' — together: 'almost general's politeness'.
segments.p2-4-g64: A repetition of 'точно' used adverbially ('as if'), introducing the following participial phrase and reinforcing the simile.
segments.p2-4-g65: 'Подавая' is another adverbial participle (imperfective: 'while/when offering'); 'руку' is the accusative object 'his hand' — the participle phrase explains the simultaneous action connected to 'оберегал'.
segments.p2-4-g66: 'Оберегал' is imperfective past 'was protecting / was guarding'; 'себя' reflexive 'himself'; 'от' is the preposition 'from'; 'чего-то' (чего + то) is an indefinite genitive meaning 'something' — the whole: 'he was, as it were, guarding himself from something.'
segments.p2-4-g67: 'Я' is the subject 'I'. 'воображал' is the masculine singular past tense of воображать (imperfective) and here means 'I imagined / I supposed'; it sets up a past, ongoing mental stance and introduces the subordinate clause that follows.
segments.p2-4-g68: Напротив is a parenthetical adverb meaning 'on the contrary' or 'instead', used here to contrast what follows with some expectation; it stands alone with a comma.
segments.p2-4-g69: Что introduces the content clause ('that ...'). 'он' is the 3rd-person pronoun 'he' (referring to the man just mentioned); together they begin the reported-thought clause.
segments.p2-4-g70: Тотчас же как = 'the moment / as soon as'. 'тотчас' is 'immediately', 'же' is an emphatic particle, and 'как' here means 'as/when' (introducing a temporal clause). 'войдет' is the perfective future 3rd-person singular of войти ('to enter'), so together: 'as soon as he enters / the moment he comes in.'
segments.p2-4-g71: Захохочет is the perfective future 3rd-person singular of захохотать: 'will burst out laughing.' 'своим прежним хохотом' is an instrumental phrase 'with his former laugh' — 'своим' is the reflexive possessive referring back to 'он', and 'прежним' qualifies 'хохотом' (instrumental case).
segments.p2-4-g72: Тоненьким is an adjective in the instrumental case agreeing with 'хохотом' and describes the quality of the laugh ('thin' or high‑pitched). 'и' = 'and'. 'со' is the variant of 'с' used before certain consonant clusters and here means 'with'. 'взвизгами' (instrumental plural) means 'with squeaks' or 'squeaky sounds' — together: 'thin and with little squeaks.'
segments.p2-4-g73: И = 'and'. 'с' + genitive 'первых' + emphatic particle 'же' + genitive 'слов' = 'from the very first words' (с here = 'from'). 'пойдут' is the future 3rd-person plural of пойти and here means 'will begin / will start to come (out)', used of jokes beginning to appear. 'плоские' modifies 'шутки' ('flat, dull'); 'его' is the possessive 'his' placed after the adjective; 'остроты' here means 'witticisms' or 'sharp remarks' (sense: keenness of wit).
segments.p2-4-g74: К ним-то = 'to them' with the particle -то added for emphasis ('it was them that...'). 'я готовился' (imperfective past) means 'I was preparing / I had been preparing (myself)'; 'еще с вечера' = 'already / since the evening' (с + genitive = 'since').
segments.p2-4-g75: Но = 'but'. 'никак уж не ожидал я' is a colloquial emphatic negation: 'I by no means / absolutely did not expect.' 'такого' is a demonstrative 'such' pointing forward to the type or degree of what follows. 'свысока' is an adverb meaning 'from on high' or 'condescendingly' (expresses attitude).
segments.p2-4-g76: Такой 'such' agrees with the feminine noun 'ласки' (genitive/accusative context here). 'превосходительной' is an adjective that literally means 'excellent' or 'superlative' but here ironizes a superior, patronizing quality ('superior, patronizing'). 'ласки' (genitive or accusative of ласка) means 'affection' or 'kindness' — in context 'a kind of patronizing affection / condescending courtesy.'
segments.p2-4-g77: Стало быть is a fixed connective meaning 'so' or 'therefore/then' used to draw a conclusion from what precedes; literally formed from 'стать' + 'быть' but functioning as a discourse particle.
segments.p2-4-g78: Он = 'he'. 'уж' is an intensifying particle here meaning 'already' or 'indeed'. 'вполне' = 'completely / entirely'. 'считал' is past tense of считать 'to consider/think' (he considered himself). 'себя' = 'himself' (reflexive). 'теперь' = 'now'. 'неизмеримо' = 'immeasurably' (adverb modifying 'выше'). 'выше меня' is the comparative 'higher/above than me'. 'во всех отношениях' is the idiom 'in every respect / in every relation' (literally 'in all relations'). The whole is a rhetorical question: 'So he already fully considered himself immeasurably above me in every respect?'
segments.p2-4-g79: Если б is the colloquial contracted form of 'если бы' used to introduce a counterfactual conditional ('if ... had'). 'он' = 'he'. 'только' = 'only / merely'. 'обидеть' is the perfective infinitive 'to offend' and here follows хотел ('wanted') to express intended action. 'меня' = 'me' (object). 'хотел' is past-tense 'wanted / intended'. 'этим генеральством' is instrumental and means 'by this generalship' or 'by this show/air of being a general' — i.e. a display of authority or superior manner.
segments.p2-4-g80: То = 'then/that' as the conditional result marker ('then, ...'). 'ничего' literally 'nothing' is used idiomatically as 'no big deal / nothing to worry about.' 'еще' here intensifies the idea: 'still / yet' — together: 'that would have been nothing yet' (i.e. not a big problem).
segments.p2-4-g81: Думал я is a parenthetical past-tense comment 'I thought' inserted after the first clause; дума́л is imperfective past of думать indicating the narrator's internal comment.
segments.p2-4-g82: 'Я бы' begins the counterfactual result ('I would have ...'). 'как-нибудь' is a common adverb meaning 'somehow' or 'in some way' (как = 'how', нибудь softens to indicate indefiniteness). 'там' is a colloquial particle here ('there' or a vague intensifier). 'отплевался' is past perfective reflexive meaning 'spat (in contempt)' or 'spat back' — used figuratively of showing contempt; the whole means 'I would have somehow spat back / spat at him.'
segments.p2-4-g142: 'просто' is an adverb of degree/modification (here: 'simply') that qualifies the predicate 'нелепо'. 'нелепо' is an impersonal predicative (adverbial or short-form adjective used predicatively) meaning 'absurd' or 'ridiculous'; the copula 'is' is omitted, forming a short nominal sentence.
segments.p2-4-g143: The phrase begins with the negating/limiting pair 'Не только' ('not only'), which introduces an addition or contrast; 'нелепо' is again the predicative adverb/adjective meaning 'absurd(ly)'. Together they form the first half of a contrastive clause: 'Not only absurd...'.
segments.p2-4-g144: 'а' here is a contrastive conjunction ('but/and'), 'и' functions as an intensifier meaning 'even/also', 'ещё' means 'more/still', and 'что-нибудь' is the indefinite pronoun formed from 'что'+'нибудь' meaning 'something/anything'; together 'а и ещё что-нибудь' yields 'but also something more/else' (i.e. 'and something else besides').
segments.p2-4-g145: This is the speech tag: 'проворчал' is a past-tense, perfective verb in 3rd person singular ('grumbled/growled'), followed by the speaker's name 'Трудолюбов' in the nominative. The comma and placement after the quoted clause are the usual way to attribute spoken words.
segments.p2-4-g146: 'наивно' is an adverb modifying the gerund 'заступаясь' (the imperfective verbal adverb meaning 'standing up for/interceding'), while 'за меня' is the preposition 'за' + accusative 'меня' meaning 'on my behalf'. The gerund indicates an action simultaneous with 'проворчал': 'grumbled, naively interceding for me.'
segments.p2-4-g147: 'Вы' is the formal or plural 'you' (here vocative/formal), followed by the particle 'уж' used as an intensifier ('indeed/really'); 'слишком' is the adverb 'too/excessively', and 'мягки' is the short-form plural nominative of the adjective 'мягкий' used predicatively with 'вы' (omitted copula): 'You are too soft/too lenient.'
segments.p2-4-g148: 'Просто' is an adverb ('simply/merely') modifying the noun 'невежливость' ('rudeness'). With no copula the phrase is a nominal sentence equivalent to 'It is simply rudeness' or 'That's simply rudeness.'
segments.p2-4-g83: 'Но' here is the contrastive conjunction 'but'. 'Что' functions as the introductory 'what' in the question 'But what if...'; together they begin a hypothetical supposition ('Но что, если...').
segments.p2-4-g84: This is the conditional introducer: 'если' = 'if'. The particle 'и' adds emphasis ('even'/'really'). 'в самом деле' is an idiom meaning 'in fact'/'indeed' (literally 'in the very matter'), so the whole phrase means 'if indeed/really'.
segments.p2-4-g85: 'без' governs the genitive and means 'without'. 'всякого желанья' = 'any desire' (всякого = 'any/whichever', желанья genitive of 'желание'). The infinitive 'обидеть' functions as the complement to 'желание' ('desire to offend'). Together: 'without any desire to offend'.
segments.p2-4-g86: Literal order is inverted: the prepositional phrase 'в его баранью башку' ('into his sheepish head') comes before the verb. 'заползла' is a feminine past tense perfective ('crept in') and agrees with the feminine subject 'идейка', which follows; 'серьезно' is an adverb modifying 'заползла'. The image is idiomatic: 'a notion crept into his head'.
segments.p2-4-g87: 'что' introduces the content clause ('that...'). 'он' is the clause subject. 'неизмеримо' is an adverb ('immeasurably'); 'выше' is the comparative 'higher/superior (to)'; with 'меня' in the genitive it means 'above me' (superior to me). 'и' links clauses; 'может' is the modal 'can/is able to' here; 'на меня смотреть' is 'to look at/regard me'; 'не иначе' = 'in no other way' (i.e. 'only'). Note that 'на' + accusative here indicates the object of the gaze/ regard.
segments.p2-4-g88: The phrase 'как только' here completes the comparative idiom 'не иначе, как только' — 'in no other way than...'; 'с покровительством' means 'with patronage/protection', i.e. 'in a patronizing or condescending manner'. Together: 'only with patronage (i.e. patronizingly)'.
segments.p2-4-g89: 'От' + genitive expresses cause or source ('from/because of'). 'От одного этого предположения' = 'from that single assumption'; 'я' is the subject; 'уже' = 'already'; 'стал' + infinitive indicates inchoative aspect 'began to...'; 'задыхаться' is the reflexive verb 'to be breathless / to begin to suffocate'.
segments.p2-4-g90: 'Я' is the subject 'I'; 'с удивлением' is an instrumental phrase meaning 'with surprise' (adverbial); 'узнал' is perfective past 'found out' or 'learned'; 'о вашем желании' is 'about your desire' (preposition 'о' + prepositional); 'участвовать с нами' is an infinitival clause 'to take part with us' (participation with the speaker group).
segments.p2-4-g91: 'Начал он' is a simple past narrative clause: 'he began.' The verb 'начал' is perfective/preterite used to introduce direct speech; 'он' is the subject pronoun 'he' (redundant for emphasis).
segments.p2-4-g92: Two present adverbial participles describe his manner of speech: 'сюсюкивая' (cooing or baby-talk murmuring, affectionate murmuring) and 'пришепетывая' (whispering/murmuring), linked by 'и' (and). They indicate how he spoke as he began.
segments.p2-4-g93: 'И растягивая слова' adds another participial phrase: 'and stretching/drawing out his words' — 'растягивая' (imperfective gerund) describes extended pronunciation; 'слова' is the plural object 'words' (here meaning utterances).
segments.p2-4-g94: 'Чего прежде с ним не бывало' is a relative/explanatory clause: 'which/that had not happened with him before.' 'Чего' is the relative pronoun 'which/that' in genitive (colloquial), 'прежде' = 'previously/before', 'с ним' = 'with him', and 'не бывало' uses the impersonal/habitual sense of 'бывать' (it had not occurred/happened) to indicate this behavior was new.
segments.p2-4-g95: 'Мы с вами' literally 'we with you' (a conversational way to address others collectively); 'как-то' (here split into 'как' + 'то') is a particle meaning 'somehow' or 'for some reason'; 'все' is an intensifier meaning 'at all'/'really'/'all together' in context; 'не встречались' is the past plural imperfective meaning 'didn't meet' / 'haven't met' (i.e. we haven't had the occasion to meet). The whole sentence is a mild, idiomatic remark that they haven't met before.
segments.p2-4-g149: Конечно is a discourse adverb meaning 'of course / naturally' placed at the sentence start and separated by a comma to signal confirmation or concession with respect to the previous statement.
segments.p2-4-g150: не negates the adjective that follows; умышленная is the full-form adjective (feminine) agreeing with an omitted feminine noun (e.g. 'невежливость' from the previous sentence) so the clause is elliptical for 'not intentional.' The adjective here functions predicatively with the noun understood.
segments.p2-4-g151: И is a connective particle ('and' / adding on) linking this exclamation to the prior remark. как это is an idiomatic phrase here meaning 'how about that' or 'what of that' (как = 'how' + это as a filler 'this/that/it'), and Симонов is a surname in the nominative used to address someone (Russian uses nominative where English would use a vocative). The clause is a short addressed comment, not a full question grammatically.
segments.p2-4-g154: гм is an interjection expressing hesitation or mild surprise ('hm' / 'hmm'). It stands alone as a phatic or thoughtful sound, not a lexical verb or adjective.
segments.p2-4-g155: Если б is the colloquial contracted conditional ('Если бы' = 'if'); the particle б attaches to mark the conditional. со мной ('with me') here means 'to/against me' in the idiom сыграли со мной ('played a trick on me'); этак is an adverb 'in this way / like that' modifying сыграли; сыграли is past-plural (they) of сыграть used in the sense 'play a trick' (here the implied subject is 'they' or 'people'). Together this is the conditional protasis: 'If they had played such a trick on me...'.
segments.p2-4-g156: заметил is the past-tense, masculine singular verb 'remarked/observed' and serves as the reporting clause naming the speaker (Ферфичкин); its word order and comma set it off as an insertion in the utterance.
segments.p2-4-g157: я is the first-person pronoun 'I'; бы here is the conditional particle left without an expressed verb, so the apodosis is elliptical ('I would' / 'I would have' with the remainder omitted). The fragmentary 'я бы' expresses a strong conditional intention while leaving the action unstated.
segments.p2-4-g120: 'Так' is an introductory adverb meaning 'so' or 'well' starting the exclamation. The clause 'вы здесь уж час' is an elliptical Russian expression where no copula is needed: literally 'you here already an hour' = 'you've been here an hour already'; 'вы' is the 2nd-person plural/formal subject, 'здесь' is the locative adverb 'here', 'уж' is an emphatic particle 'already/indeed', and 'час' is the time noun 'hour'.
segments.p2-4-g121: The single exclamation 'ах' is an interjection expressing sympathy or surprise; punctuated separately to mark a compassionate or mocking cry addressed to the addressee.
segments.p2-4-g122: 'бедный' is an adjective used substantively in direct address ('poor one' / 'poor fellow') and functions as an exclamatory epithet modifying the person being spoken to.
segments.p2-4-g123: Вскрикнул is a perfective past tense verb, 3rd person singular, 'cried out/shrieked'; the adverb 'насмешливо' ('mockingly') modifies the verb. Word order is inverted (verb–adverb–subject), so 'Зверков' is the subject (the speaker who cried out).
segments.p2-4-g124: Потому что is the causal conjunction 'because' split into two words here: 'потому' ('for that reason') + 'что' (the complementizer 'that/that...'), introducing the subordinate clause explaining his laugh.
segments.p2-4-g125: По + dative ('его понятиям') forms the phrase 'по его понятиям' meaning 'according to his notions / in his view'; 'по' here is 'according to', 'его' is the possessive pronoun 'his', and 'понятиям' is dative plural of 'понятие' ('notions'/'understanding').
segments.p2-4-g126: 'Это' is the demonstrative pronoun 'this/it' referring to the situation; 'действительно' is an adverb 'indeed/really'. The phrase 'должно было быть' is a periphrastic past modal: 'должно' (impersonal modal 'ought to') + past auxiliary 'было' + infinitive 'быть' yields 'ought to have been' / 'should have been'. 'Ужасно' is an intensifying adverb 'terribly/very', and 'смешно' is the predicative 'funny'.
segments.p2-4-g127: 'За ним' is a prepositional phrase with 'за' + pronoun in instrumental, functioning adverbially as 'after him' or 'behind him' (i.e., following his action); 'ним' is the instrumental form of 'он' ('him').
segments.p2-4-g128: 'Подленьким' is a diminutive/pejorative adjective in the instrumental singular agreeing with the forthcoming noun 'голоском'; here it describes the manner of speaking: 'in a mean/petty little ... voice.'
segments.p2-4-g129: 'Звонким' is another instrumental adjective agreeing with 'голоском', meaning 'ringing' or 'clear-sounding' and further characterizing the voice's quality.
segments.p2-4-g130: 'Как' is the comparative particle 'like/as'; 'у' plus noun in genitive ('у собачонки') indicates possession ('of the little dog' / 'like a puppy's'), so the phrase means 'in a voice like a puppy's.'
segments.p2-4-g131: 'Голоском' is the instrumental noun 'with a little voice' marking manner; 'закатился' is past tense (masculine) of 'закатиться' used figuratively here—'rolled out' or 'piped up' in that voice. 'Подлец' is the nominative noun 'scoundrel' (the subject) and 'Ферфичкин' is the character's name; the word order is inverted (manner first, then verb, then subject).
segments.p2-4-g132: This whole clause is an impersonal construction: 'Очень уж' is an emphatic intensifier ('very/indeed'), 'и' before 'ему' means 'even' ('and/also' used for emphasis), and 'ему' is dative 'to him'. 'Показалось' is the past impersonal verb 'it seemed' (literally 'it appeared to him'), while 'смешно и конфузно' are predicative adverbial/short adjectives 'funny and embarrassing'. 'Мое положение' (my situation) is the logical subject placed after the predicate (inverted word order).
segments.p2-4-g178: 'Ведь' is a modal particle meaning 'after all' that gives the clause a justificatory tone. The clause 'я вашей квартиры не знал' uses 'вашей квартиры' (genitive) as the thing not known and 'не знал' is past-tense negation 'didn't know' — i.e. 'I wasn't acquainted with/wasn't aware of your apartment (its location)'. Word order is neutral; the genitive nominal phrase stands as the object of знать.
segments.p2-4-g179: 'Где ж' is an emphatic interrogative ('where on earth/then'), with 'ж' adding force or impatience. 'Вас' is the accusative/object 'you', and 'отыскивать' is the imperfective verb 'to search for/locate' (imperfective here expresses the general act of trying to find you). The whole phrase is a rhetorical question linked to the previous clause (explaining why he couldn't find you).
segments.p2-4-g180: 'Оборотился' is the past tense (perfective) of 'оборотиться' meaning 'turned (around)' — here a sudden physical turn; placing 'он' after the verb is a common word-order option in Russian narration. 'Ко мне' (with preposition 'ко', a variant of 'к' used before certain consonants) is a dative-direction phrase 'toward me', and 'вдруг' means 'suddenly'.
segments.p2-4-g181: 'Но опять как-то' = 'but again somehow' (here 'как-то' functions as an adverb 'somehow/sort of'). The gerund 'не глядя' (negative adverbial participle) means 'without looking'; 'на меня' is the prepositional combination meaning 'at me' ('на' here 'to/toward'). So the phrase says he turned to the speaker yet again without actually looking at him.
segments.p2-4-g182: 'Очевидно' here is a parenthetical adverb meaning 'apparently' or 'it seems', marking the speaker's inference about the motive or state of the other man.
segments.p2-4-g183: 'Он имел что-то против' uses the verb 'иметь' in the past ('he had') with 'против' following — a fixed construction meaning 'to have something against (someone/something)' i.e. 'to object'. 'Что-то' is the indefinite pronoun 'something'. The object of the objection is left unspecified (elliptical).
segments.p2-4-g184: Sentence-initial 'Знать' is a colloquial particle meaning roughly 'it seems' or 'one can assume' (used to introduce a conjecture). It is not the full lexical verb 'to know' here but a modal/expletive comment.
segments.p2-4-g185: 'После вчерашнего' is an elliptical temporal noun phrase 'after yesterday's (events)' with 'после' + genitive, and 'надумался' is the perfective reflexive past 'he decided / had second thoughts / changed his mind'. Together they mean 'he came to that decision after what happened yesterday.'
segments.p2-4-g186: Все is the plural pronoun 'everyone/all' as the subject. Сели is the past tense, plural form of сесть ('to sit down'), so the phrase means 'everyone sat down' with normal subject‑verb order; the semicolon gives a brief pause before the narrator's remark.
segments.p2-4-g187: Сел is the past singular masculine form of сесть ('I sat' here, narrator male), placed before the pronoun for a slightly emphatic or narrative effect. И functions as 'also/too' (not just a simple coordinating 'and'), and я is the first‑person pronoun; together 'сел и я' = 'I sat down too.'
segments.p2-4-g188: Стол is the noun 'table' in nominative; был is the past tense, third person singular of быть, functioning as the copula; круглый is a predicative adjective in nominative singular masculine agreeing with стол. The clause is a simple past descriptive statement.
segments.p2-4-g189: По левую руку от меня is a colloquial spatial phrase meaning 'to the left of me' (по + accusative construction indicating position). Пришелся is the past form of прийтись used impersonally here to mean 'fell/ended up' or 'was placed' (i.e., 'came to be on my left'), and Трудолюбов is the nominative proper name that is the logical subject of that placement.
segments.p2-4-g190: По правую is an elliptical repetition of 'по правую руку' ('to the right [hand]') with the noun руку omitted but understood. The name Симонов stands in nominative; the verb is omitted as parallel to the previous clause, so the meaning is 'to my right was Simonov.'
segments.p2-4-g191: Зверков is a proper name in the nominative (here a surname) serving as the subject; сел is past singular masculine of сесть ('sat down'); напротив is an adverb meaning 'opposite' or 'across from', indicating location relative to the speaker or the table.
segments.p2-4-g192: Ферфичкин is a name in the nominative; подле is a preposition meaning 'near' or 'beside' that governs the pronoun него in the genitive; together 'Ферфичкин подле него' = 'Ferfichkin beside him.'
segments.p2-4-g193: Между takes two coordinated nouns in the appropriate case to mean 'between.' Ним is the pronoun in the instrumental form here ('him'), и is the conjunction 'and', and Трудолюбовым is the surname in the instrumental — all together 'between him and Trudolyubov.'
segments.p2-4-g160: Да here is a discourse particle/introductory exclamation (like English “well” or “you could have”), not a simple affirmative; вы + бы + велели is a conditional construction: бы with the past tense велели yields 'you would have ordered/had [something] ordered'. себе is a dative reflexive meaning 'for yourself', что-нибудь (что + нибудь) is an indefinite pronoun meaning 'something', and подать is the infinitive object of велели ('to have something brought/served').
segments.p2-4-g161: Перебил is the past tense (masculine singular) of перебить used here in the reporting clause meaning 'interrupted'. The following name (Зверков) identifies the speaker; the verb precedes the name as a normal narrative reporting construction.
segments.p2-4-g162: Или introduces an alternative clause. Просто is an adverb 'simply/just' modifying спросили бы; спросили is past tense plural used with the conditional particle бы to form 'you would have asked/requested'. Обедать is an infinitive meaning 'to dine' (here 'to ask to dine' / 'to ask that dinner begin'); не дожидаясь is a деепричастие (gerund) 'without waiting', functioning as an adverbial: 'without waiting'.
segments.p2-4-g163: Согласитесь is the 2nd-person plural or polite imperative/form of согласиться used as a request/command meaning 'agree' or 'admit (it)'; it's often used to prompt assent from the listeners before the speaker states a claim.
segments.p2-4-g164: Что introduces an indirect statement 'that ...'. Я бы мог uses the conditional particle бы with the past form мог (from мочь) to mean 'I could have' or 'I would have been able to'; это is a demonstrative pronoun 'it/this' as the direct object of сделать ('do/make'), and без всякого позволения is a prepositional phrase 'without any permission' (всякого = 'any' here, possessive/genitive with без).
segments.p2-4-g165: Отрезал я is a narrative reporting clause; отрезал is past tense (masculine) of отрезать used metaphorically here to mean 'said curtly/abruptly' (literally 'cut [in/it] off'), and я is the subject 'I' placed after the verb for stylistic brevity.
segments.p2-4-g166: Если is the subordinating conjunction 'if'. Я ждал is the past tense (masculine singular) of ждать, meaning 'I waited' or 'I was waiting'; the clause sets up a conditional/contestable fact about the speaker's waiting.
segments.p2-4-g167: То is the correlated particle often used with если: when sent alone as here it yields an elliptical 'then' or a trailing conclusion ('then so it is' / 'so be it'), leaving the rest unsaid; it's a discourse particle closing the thought.
segments.p2-4-g170: Садимся is 1st-person plural present (or hortative) of садиться used as an exhortation 'let's sit down' or 'we're sitting down' — a common way to invite people to take their seats.
segments.p2-4-g171: Господа is the vocative/plural form addressing the assembled men as 'gentlemen' (a polite form of address).
segments.p2-4-g172: Закричал is past tense (masculine) of закричать 'cried out' or 'shouted', and вошедший is a participle from войти meaning 'having entered' or 'the entering one' that modifies the name Симонов; together they form the reporting clause 'cried the entering Simonov' (i.e. 'cried Simonov as he entered').
segments.p2-4-g173: Все готово is a fixed announcement 'everything is ready', where все = 'everything' and готово is a short-form adjective/predicate meaning 'ready'.
segments.p2-4-g174: За шампанское отвечаю is an idiomatic clause: за + accusative here means 'for' in the sense 'as for the champagne' or 'regarding the champagne', and отвечаю is present tense 'I answer / I am responsible' meaning 'I vouch for' or 'I'll take responsibility (for quality/payment)'; шампанское is the noun 'champagne'.
segments.p2-4-g175: Отлично is an adverb 'excellently/perfectly' modifying the short passive form заморожено ('frozen/chilled'); заморожено is a short passive/adjectival form meaning 'is chilled/frozen' describing the champagne's state.
segments.p2-4-g243: Я is the first-person singular nominative pronoun 'I'. ужасно is an adverbial intensifier (here meaning 'very'/'terribly') modifying the verb. краснел is the past-tense, imperfective form (masculine singular ending -л) of 'to blush/redden', indicating a past, ongoing or repeated action; standard S-V word order places the subject before the verb.
segments.p2-4-g244: Небогато is a predicative adverb/adjective formed with the negative prefix не ('not') meaning 'not rich' or 'poorly' used here as a brief evaluative comment; the initial dash marks reported speech.
segments.p2-4-g245: важно is an adverb meaning 'importantly' or 'with dignity' placed before the verb to qualify how the remark was made. заметил is the past tense (masculine singular) of заметить 'to remark/notice' — here 'remarked'. Зверков is the speaker's name in nominative; Russian often places verb before subject in reported speech (V S order).
segments.p2-4-g246: Да‑с is a colloquial interjection: a drawn-out Да ('yes') with a following s-sound; here it functions as a conversational 'yes' or 'well' and is not the preposition с. The hyphen shows the drawn-out pronunciation.
segments.p2-4-g247: нельзя is an impersonal predicate meaning 'it is not allowed/one mustn't/you can't'; it governs the infinitive that follows. в + кафе-ресторанах is a locative expression ('in café-restaurants') — кафе is indeclinable here and ресторанах is the prepositional plural of 'ресторан' specifying the type of place. обедать is the infinitive 'to dine' and completes the impersonal construction нельзя + инфинитив.
segments.p2-4-g248: нагло is an adverb meaning 'brazenly' or 'impudently' that modifies the following verb. прибавил is past tense masculine singular of прибавить 'to add'; in speech-reporting it means 'added (as a remark)'. Ферфичкин is the proper name in nominative; the verb-before-subject order (V S) is common in reported dialogue.
segments.p2-4-g194: This is an elongated, drawn-out spelling of the imperative verb 'скажите' (to say/tell), written with dashes and extra vowels to show stuttering or emphasis in speech. Grammatically it is the 2nd person plural/formal imperative (скажите) used to address someone politely or to a group; the dashes mark the beginning of a spoken utterance.
segments.p2-4-g195: The single pronoun 'вы' (you) immediately follows the imperative as the addressee; here it functions as a vocative/direct address (2nd person plural or formal singular). The period after it is a pause in the interrupted speech.
segments.p2-4-g198: A short interrogative fragment: preposition 'в' + locative/instrumental form 'департаменте' forming 'in the department'. The question mark shows it's a brief question (elliptical sentence) asking whether something (previously mentioned) is in the department.
segments.p2-4-g199: The past-tense verb 'продолжал' (he continued) plus the reflexive/verb phrase 'заниматься' indicates an ongoing action in the past; here it frames that Zverkov kept doing something. 'Продолжал' is imperfective past (he was continuing).
segments.p2-4-g200: 'Видя' is a gerund (деепричастие) from 'видеть' and functions like 'seeing' or 'noticing' — it gives the circumstance for the main clause that follows. It links temporally/cause-wise to the main verb 'вообразил'.
segments.p2-4-g201: A subordinate clause introduced by 'что' (that): 'я' is the subject 'I', and 'сконфужен' is a short-form passive/adjective meaning 'embarrassed' (result state). Together: 'that I was embarrassed.'
segments.p2-4-g202: 'Он' (he) is the subject; 'серьезно' (seriously) is an adverb modifying the verb; 'вообразил' is past-tense perfective 'imagined' or 'supposed' — here meaning he came to believe or conceived seriously that something was needed.
segments.p2-4-g203: Another subordinate clause with 'что' (that). 'Меня' is the accusative 'me' as the object of the infinitive action; 'надо' expresses necessity ('one must' / 'it is necessary to'); 'обласкать' is a perfective infinitive meaning 'to shower with affection / to pet'; 'и' simply connects to the following infinitive. Grammatically this whole phrase says 'that it was necessary to lavish affection on me and ...'.
segments.p2-4-g204: 'Так сказать' is a fixed parenthetical idiom meaning 'so to speak' or 'as it were'; here it softens or comments on the following verb. Grammatically it's an adverbial phrase set off by commas.
segments.p2-4-g205: The infinitive 'ободрить' (perfective) completes the earlier connected infinitives and means 'to cheer up / to encourage' — so the pair 'обласкать ... ободрить' lists actions Zverkov imagined were needed. As perfective infinitives, they present the intended one-time acts.
segments.p2-4-g237: A short interrogative: Какое (neuter nominative interrogative adjective) modifies содержание and asks "what kind/kind of"; это is a demonstrative pronoun placed between adjective and noun for emphasis/clarification (literal: "what is this maintenance"); there is no copula in Russian, so the three words form a direct question about the nature of the 'содержание'.
segments.p2-4-g238: То есть is the set phrase meaning "that is (to say)" used to rephrase the previous word (recasting 'содержание' as 'жалованье'); the doubled consonant with a hyphen (ж-жалованье) marks a stutter in speech rather than a separate grammatical element; жалованье (nominative) is an older/colloquial form meaning 'salary/pay', here offered as the reinterpretation.
segments.p2-4-g239: Да is an exclamatory particle here (colloquial interjection/softener) introducing the protest; что functions as an exclamatory particle meaning roughly "what/how (d'you)"; вы (2nd person plural/formal) is the subject; меня (accusative) is the direct object placed before the verb for emphasis; экзаменуете is present tense, 2nd person plural of экзаменовать used colloquially = "to quiz/interrogate".
segments.p2-4-g240: Впрочем is a sentential adverb meaning "however/anyway" that signals either a correction or return to the speaker's point; here it functions like English "anyway" to continue the narrative.
segments.p2-4-g241: Я is the subject 'I'; тут же is an adverbial phrase meaning "right away/immediately" (тут 'at this point/right here' + же emphatic particle giving immediacy); и is an additive/emphatic particle here ("even/indeed") and назвал is past tense, perfective, meaning "named/told" (i.e., I stated the amount).
segments.p2-4-g242: This subordinate clause is introduced by сколько ('how much'), which governs a genitive expression of quantity; получаю is present tense, 1st person singular of получать meaning here 'I receive/get (as pay)'; жалованья is the genitive of жалованье (archaic/colloquial for 'pay/salary') and is the thing being measured by сколько.
segments.p2-4-g230: Ферфичкин is a proper name in the nominative (the subject). фыркнул is the past tense, masculine, singular form of the perfective verb фыркнуть — a simple past action: “(he) snorted.” The sentence is a concise subject+verb clause describing a single past action.
segments.p2-4-g231: Симонов is the subject (surname). иронически is an adverb modifying the verb, meaning “ironically.” посмотрел is the past tense (perfective) of посмотреть, here meaning “looked (at).” на меня (preposition + accusative) marks the target of the look.
segments.p2-4-g232: Трудолюбов is the subject. остановился + infinitive (остановился есть) means he “stopped (doing something),” here “stopped eating,” with есть as the infinitive “to eat.” и стал + infinitive expresses beginning to do something (here стал рассматривать — “began to examine/look at”). меня is the direct object of рассматривать, and с любопытством (instrumental) is an adverbial phrase “with curiosity.”
segments.p2-4-g233: Зверкова is a feminine surname in the nominative; pokorobilo is a past neuter form used impersonally to mean “it put (someone) out/irritated.” Russian often places the affected person after such an impersonal verb: “Zverkova was displeased/put out.”
segments.p2-4-g234: но is the conjunction “but.” он is the pronoun subject “he.” не хотел + infinitive (заметить) expresses unwillingness or refusal in the past — “he did not want to notice.” заметить is a perfective infinitive meaning “to notice/remark.”
segments.p2-4-g235: Ну-у-у is an elongated interjection (ну) expressing hesitation or a drawled ‘well…’. It’s not functioning here as a preposition — the repetition stretches the sound to signal pause or prompting.
segments.p2-4-g236: а is a contrastive/connecting particle here introducing the question (“and/so”). как is the interrogative “how/what about.” ваше is the possessive adjective “your,” agreeing in gender/number with содержание, and содержание (neuter noun) here means “maintenance/support” (money/allowance), so the phrase asks about the person’s means of support.
segments.p2-4-g206: "Что ж он," is a colloquial opening to a rhetorical question; Что (what/what's) with the particle ж adding emphasis or mild impatience, and он (he) is the subject placed after the phrase for a slightly brusque tone.
segments.p2-4-g207: хочет is the 3rd person singular present of хотеть (to want) and here forms the core of the rhetorical question "does he want..."; the comma shows a short pause before the tag that follows.
segments.p2-4-g208: что ли is a colloquial question tag meaning roughly "or what / I wonder"; ли is the enclitic 'whether' and together the pair softens or questions the assertion rather than introducing a subordinate clause.
segments.p2-4-g209: чтоб introduces a subjunctive-like content clause ('that I should ...'); я is the first-person subject; в + него indicates direction/target ('at him' or 'into him'); бутылкой is the instrumental noun ('with a bottle'—the instrument); пустил is past tense masculine of пустить ('to send/release/let fly') here colloquially meaning 'to throw (at)'. The whole phrase expresses the speaker's imagined violent act.
segments.p2-4-g210: подумал я is a simple past narration with the verb before the pronoun for a slightly emphatic or literary word order; в бешенстве is an adverbial phrase ('in fury/angry') specifying the speaker's mental state while thinking.
segments.p2-4-g211: Раздражался is past imperfective reflexive (I was becoming / got irritated) expressing a process or tendency; the subject я follows the verb for stylistic emphasis.
segments.p2-4-g212: с непривычки is an idiomatic causal phrase: с (from) + непривычки (lack of habit / not being used to it), giving the reason for the irritation.
segments.p2-4-g213: как-то (literally 'somehow' or 'rather') here moderates the following adjective; неестественно is an adverb 'unnaturally' and скоро in this context means 'quickly' (speed of becoming irritated). Together: 'somehow unnaturally quickly.'
segments.p2-4-g214: В is the preposition 'in/into' presented here as an abrupt, truncated utterance (the rest of the phrase follows in the next clause). Its exact meaning is determined by the following words, but standing alone it signals a locative/directional start.
segments.p2-4-g217: й here is a colloquial particle/phonetic residue attached in the printed split utterance; context shows the intended phrase is 'в канцелярии' — 'in the office.' Канцелярии is the prepositional singular of канцелярия (office/bureau), indicating location after the (implied) в.
segments.p2-4-g218: ответил я is the past-tense verb 'I answered' with the subject after the verb (inverted word order typical in speech); отрывисто is an adverb ('abruptly/curtly') describing how he spoke.
segments.p2-4-g219: глядя is a present adverbial participle ('looking') that indicates a concurrent action by the subject; в тарелку uses в + accusative to mean 'at the plate' (direction of gaze), so the phrase reads 'while looking at the plate.'
segments.p2-4-g303: Довольно here is an interjection meaning 'enough' or 'that's enough', not the adverb 'quite'. As the opening word of the utterance it functions like an imperative admonition; the comma shows the address continues.
segments.p2-4-g304: господа is the vocative-like address formed from господин (nominative plural used to address a group). It means 'gentlemen' and is set off by commas as a direct addressee; grammatically it behaves like a nominative plural used for calling out to people.
segments.p2-4-g305: довольно repeated at the end is the same interjection 'enough' used for emphasis; the exclamation mark intensifies the command-like force. Repetition is a common rhetorical device to close or strengthen a rebuke.
segments.p2-4-g306: закричал is a past-tense, perfective verb (masculine singular) meaning 'cried out' or 'shouted' — it marks a sudden loud utterance. всевластно is an adverbial use of всевластный ('all‑powerful') meaning 'imperiously' or 'with full authority'. The order (verb + adverb + name) places the action first for narrative effect; Зверков is the nominative surname of the speaker. The initial dash marks the reporting tag for the preceding direct speech.
segments.p2-4-g307: Как here is the exclamative 'how'. Это functions as an expletive pronoun ('it/this') used to point to the judged situation rather than a concrete noun. Глупо is the short/adverbial form from глупый used predicatively ('stupid' / 'foolish'); together the clause literally means 'How this is stupid' → 'How stupid it is!'.
segments.p2-4-g308: проворчал is a past-tense, masculine singular verb meaning 'grumbled' or 'muttered' — a low-voiced complaint. Симонов is the nominative surname of the speaker. The verb-before-name order is a common narrative inversion (action first) and the dash shows this is the speech attribution.
segments.p2-4-g249: По-моему is a parenthetical opinion phrase built from the preposition по + dative (моему). It functions like English “in my opinion” and is set off by a comma; the preposition carries the sense “according to / as for.”
segments.p2-4-g250: This is a sequence of adverbs modifying the predicate: так ‘so/thus’ sets the general degree, даже ‘even’ adds emphasis, просто ‘simply/just’ downplays it, and бедно (an adverbial/predicative form of бедный) means ‘poor’ or ‘meagre’ — together: ‘so even simply poor / just plain poor.’ Word order is the natural stacking of modifiers before the predicative adjective.
segments.p2-4-g251: серьезно is an adverb modifying the past-tense verb заметил (perfective past, masculine singular) meaning ‘remarked.’ The proper name Трудолюбов is the nominative subject placed after the verb (a common Russian word order for reported speech: verb then speaker).
segments.p2-4-g252: И here is the coordinating particle 'and' that adds a connective or slight exclamatory flavor. как introduces an exclamatory clause meaning ‘how’ (not a comparative), вы is the polite/plural 'you', and похудели is the past tense plural (or formal singular) of ‘to lose weight’ used as an exclamation: ‘how you've lost weight.’
segments.p2-4-g253: A second short exclamative clause with как ‘how’ + перeменились (past plural of перемениться) meaning ‘have changed/been transformed.’ The repetition of как emphasizes surprise at the change.
segments.p2-4-g256: с тех пор is a fixed temporal phrase meaning ‘since then.’ Grammatically, с is the preposition 'from/since' and тех is the genitive plural/demonstrative (from тот) required after с; пор (from пора) here denotes 'time/occasion' and completes the idiom.
segments.p2-4-g259: прибавил is the past tense (masculine singular) of прибавить; here it means ‘added’ in speech (i.e., added as he spoke). The following name (Зверков) is the speaker’s name in nominative and follows the verb.
segments.p2-4-g260: уже 'already' + не 'not' + без 'without' + яду (genitive of яд). The phrase уже не без яду is idiomatic: literally ‘no longer without poison,’ i.e. ‘not without a bit of venom/bitter irony.’ Note яду appears in a non-standard/older form (genitive) in this idiom.
segments.p2-4-g261: с каким-то is a prepositional phrase with с + instrumental какой-то meaning ‘with some kind of…’; нахальным is an adjective in the instrumental agreeing with сожалением (also instrumental), so together 'with some kind of impudent/brazen regret.' The particle-то that combines with каким is the indefinite particle making it 'some,' treated here as part of the phrase.
segments.p2-4-g262: рассматривая is a деепричастие (adverbial participle) 'looking at / examining' that attaches to the main clause to indicate manner; it takes меня и мой костюм as its objects. Меня is accusative 'me'; мой костюм (accusative identical to nominative for inanimate nouns) is 'my suit.'
segments.p2-4-g263: "Да полно конфузить-то," is a colloquial exclamation: Да here is a particle/introductory exclamation (an emphatic 'come on' / 'oh'), полно is an interjection meaning 'enough' and конфузить is the imperfective verb 'to embarrass' used in an infinitive-like phrase. The enclitic -то on конфузить is a colloquial particle adding emphasis or brusque dismissal ('—enough with the embarrassing, already').
segments.p2-4-g264: хихикая is the adverbial participle (деепричастие) of хихикать and describes the manner of the action: 'giggling' or 'while giggling.' It modifies the main past action that follows.
segments.p2-4-g265: вскрикнул is a past tense, perfective verb (masculine singular) meaning 'cried out' or 'shrieked' and names the main speech act; the proper name Ферфичкин follows in the nominative as the subject/agent. Word order places the speech-framing verb before the name for slightly brusque or narrative effect.
segments.p2-4-g266: Милостивый государь is a formal, somewhat ironical form of address: Милостивый (an honorific adjective used as a vocative) + государь (lord/sir) together function as 'gracious sir.' The two words form a fixed polite address.
segments.p2-4-g267: знайте is the second-person plural (or polite) imperative of знать, used here as 'know' or 'be advised' — a formal command/request to take notice of what follows.
segments.p2-4-g268: что introduces a subordinate content clause ('that ...'); я is the nominative subject 'I'; не is the negation particle; конфужусь is the present tense, reflexive verb (конфузиться) meaning 'I am embarrassed' (the reflexive ending -ся marks the stative/experiential sense). Together: 'that I am not embarrassed.'
segments.p2-4-g269: прорвался is past tense, perfective, used idiomatically here as 'broke through' or 'burst out' (i.e. 'I blurted out'); я is the subject pronoun 'I' (often explicit in Russian for emphasis); наконец is an adverb 'finally' modifying прорвался.
segments.p2-4-g270: слышите is present-second-person plural used as an interjection 'you hear!' or 'do you hear!'; the trailing -с is a colloquial enclitic particle (here written separately) that adds brusque emphasis/attitude (not a prepositional 'с').
segments.p2-4-g271: Я is the nominative subject 'I'; обедаю is the first-person singular present of обедать, 'I dine' or 'I am dining'; здесь is an adverb 'here' specifying place. The clause is a plain present-stative statement.
segments.p2-4-g272: в + кафе-ресторане is a prepositional phrase (в + prepositional case) meaning 'in the café-restaurant.' The hyphenated кафе-ресторане is stylistic/ironic naming (and the phrase is in quotes in the original to signal a tone of mockery or specificity).
segments.p2-4-g273: на свои деньги uses на + accusative to express 'at the expense of' or 'with' (i.e. 'with my own money'); свои is the reflexive/possessive pronoun agreeing with the subject (here 'my/one's own'); деньги is the noun 'money' in the plural accusative (form identical to nominative).
segments.p2-4-g274: на свои repeats the preposition + possessive to emphasize 'my own' (elliptical: 'on my own [money]') — the noun is omitted and understood from the previous phrase.
segments.p2-4-g275: а не на чужие is a contrasting clause: а ('but') sets up the contrast, не negates, на чужие repeats the preposition + adjective 'another's/other people's' (чужие) to mean 'not on others' money.' The adjective чужие agrees in plural and stands nominally.
segments.p2-4-g276: заметьте is the polite/plural imperative of заметить 'notice' or 'mind' used to call attention — 'note this' or 'mind that'; это is the demonstrative pronoun 'this/it' as the direct object.
segments.p2-4-g277: monsieur Ферфичкин is an address combining the French title monsieur ('mister') with the proper name Ферфичкин in the vocative sense — a polite/ironic direct address to the character.
segments.p2-4-g278: This is a drawn-out exclamation written with a hyphen to show lengthening. 'Ка' is an emphatic/exclamatory particle and 'ак' is an old colloquial/folkloric particle (akin to 'so'/'thus') that together form a surprised or indignant cry; the form is dialectal/expressive rather than grammatical.
segments.p2-4-g220: A single coordinating conjunction used as a one-word, elliptical utterance; here 'И' functions like the English 'And?' (an abbreviated prompt or challenge). The punctuation and intonation (a dash before the word in the printed line) mark it as a brief spoken rejoinder rather than a full clause.
segments.p2-4-g223: "Ввам" is a (misspelled) dative pronoun meaning 'to/for you' (standard form вам); "ввыгодно" is an impersonal predicate meaning 'advantageous' or 'profitable.' Together they form an impersonal question 'Вам выгодно?' literally 'To you (is it) advantageous?'. The unusual doubled letters reflect transcription noise but do not change the grammar.
segments.p2-4-g224: This is the imperative verb 'Скажите' (please tell me) broken with a hyphen to indicate a drawn-out or broken pronunciation ('Ска-ажите'). It's the polite/plural imperative used to request information; the hyphenation is a performative cue (stutter/drawl), not a different verb form.
segments.p2-4-g225: 'Что' here introduces an indirect question ('what'); 'вас' is the accusative object 'you' (the person affected); 'паанудило' is a corrupted/archaic spelling of a past-tense verb meaning 'compelled' (used impersonally with an object), and it takes the following infinitive 'оставить' ('to leave') as its complement. The whole clause asks what it was that compelled you to quit your previous post.
segments.p2-4-g226: 'То' is a demonstrative pronoun ('that') and 'и' here reinforces/emphasizes the demonstrative ('that very'); the following hyphenated cluster 'па-а-анудило' is a stretched, transcribed form of the past-tense verb (panudilo) meaning 'compelled' — the hyphenation marks the speaker drawing out the syllables. Grammatically this is a short main clause: 'То и панудило' = 'That was what compelled (me)'.
segments.p2-4-g227: 'Что' introduces the subordinate explanatory clause ('that'); 'захотелось' is an impersonal past form meaning 'it came about that one wanted' or 'one felt like,' and it governs the infinitive 'оставить' ('to leave'). 'Прежнюю службу' is the direct object phrase 'the former service/post.' The clause explains the content of what 'compelled' him.
segments.p2-4-g228: 'Протянул' is past masculine of 'протянуть' here meaning 'drew out' or 'prolonged' (one's words); 'я' is the subject (postverbal for stylistic effect), and 'втрое больше' is an adverbial phrase 'three times more/longer' modifying how he spoke. The clause reports the manner of utterance: he elongated the phrase threefold.
segments.p2-4-g229: 'Уже почти не владея собою' is a de‑participial adverbial phrase (деепричастие) meaning 'by now, almost not possessing/mastering himself,' i.e. 'almost beside himself.' 'Не владея' is the negative gerund 'not being master of,' and 'собою' is the reflexive instrumental 'of himself' or 'over himself.'
segments.p2-4-g293: "Вы" is the polite/plural 2nd-person pronoun used here to address someone directly; it sits alone with a comma as a vocative-like fronting of the addressee.
segments.p2-4-g294: "кажется" is the impersonal present form of казаться, used parenthetically to mean "it seems" or "apparently," qualifying the whole clause that follows.
segments.p2-4-g295: The verb "намереваетесь" is 2nd-person plural/formal present of намереваться meaning "intend," followed by the infinitive "показывать". The object "ваш ум" (your intellect/mind) precedes the infinitive — a colloquial word order meaning "intend to show (off) your intellect." The infinitive is imperfective, implying the general intention rather than a single completed act.
segments.p2-4-g296: "Не беспокойтесь" is a negative imperative in the 2nd-person plural/formal (reflexive verb беспокоиться) meaning "don't worry" or "do not be concerned."
segments.p2-4-g297: This is a conditional clause: "это" = "this/it," "было бы" = past stem + particle бы forming the conditional "would be," and the predicate is the adverb + adjective sequence "совершенно здесь лишнее" meaning "completely unnecessary here." Word order places the adverb and locative adverb before the predicate adjective for emphasis.
segments.p2-4-g298: "Да" here is an interjection/intensifier at the start of an exclamation; "вы это что" is a colloquial cluster meaning roughly "what are you doing/what's this of yours?" with "что" functioning as an exclamatory particle.
segments.p2-4-g299: "сударь" is an old-fashioned polite address ("sir"); "вы мой" is a colloquial, slightly familiar tag (literally "you my") often used to soften or mock the address—word order is emphatic and idiomatic.
