segments.p2-4-g566: Ни is an emphatic negative particle ('not / not even', sense 0) that combines with the prepositional phrase за что to form an idiom meaning 'not for anything' or 'by no means.' За here means 'for' (sense 1) and что is the pronoun 'what/anything' (sense 1) within that idiom.
segments.p2-4-g567: Нарочно is an adverb 'deliberately' (sense 0). Буду is the future auxiliary of быть (sense 0) forming a periphrastic future with the infinitives сидеть ('to sit / stay', sense 0) and пить ('to drink' — here likely alcohol, sense 1). До + конца is the prepositional phrase 'until the end' with до meaning 'until/up to' (sense 0) and конца the genitive of конец 'end' (sense 0).
segments.p2-4-g568: В знак того is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'as a sign of that (fact)'; в is 'in'/'as' (sense 0), знак is 'sign' or 'token' (sense 0), and того is the genitive of 'that' pointing forward to the clause introduced by что (sense 0).
segments.p2-4-g569: Что introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. Не is simple negation (sense 0). Придаю is the 1st-person present of придавать used here in the sense 'to attribute/attach (importance)' (sense 2). Вам is the dative 'to you' (sense 0). Ни is the reinforcing negative particle 'not even' (sense 0) paired with малейшей to produce 'not the slightest'. Малейшей is the genitive (or instrumental in some constructions) of малейший, here 'slightest / tiniest / least' (sense 2: insignificant/minor). Важности is the genitive of важность 'importance' (sense 0). Together: 'that I do not attach the slightest importance to you.'
segments.p2-4-g570: Буду + infinitives сидеть и пить forms the future ('I will sit and drink'); буду is the auxiliary 'will be' (sense 0), сидеть 'to sit/stay' (sense 0), and пить 'to drink' (here context implies drinking alcohol, sense 1).
segments.p2-4-g571: Потому что is the causal conjunction 'because' (потому 'for that reason', sense 0; что 'that', sense 0). Здесь is the locative adverb 'here' (sense 0). Кабак is a colloquial word for 'tavern'/'beer-house' (sense 0).
segments.p2-4-g572: А is a contrasting conjunction 'and / but' (sense 0) linking the justification; я is the subject 'I' (sense 0). Деньги here means 'money' (general, sense 1). За marks purpose/price 'for' (sense 1) and вход is 'entrance/entry' (sense 0). Заплатил is the perfective past 'paid' (sense 0). The clause means 'and I paid money for the entrance.'
segments.p2-4-g612: Они is the nominative plural subject 'they'. слушали is the past-tense, imperfective, plural verb 'listened' (regular past form indicating repeated or ongoing past action). его is the accusative object 'him' placed before the verb (neutral word order). чуть не functions as the adverbial phrase 'almost' (чуть + не together form the idiom 'almost'), and с благоговеньем is a prepositional phrase with с + instrumental (благоговеньем) indicating manner: 'with reverence.'
segments.p2-4-g613: Видно is an impersonal adverb/predicate meaning 'apparently' or 'it is visible/clear'; было is the past neuter form of быть used here to form an impersonal past construction ('it was apparent'). Together they create the phrase 'it was apparent/clear.'
segments.p2-4-g614: что introduces a subordinate content clause 'that ...'. его is the accusative pronoun 'him' (here functioning as the logical subject of the clause in a construction that in English is naturally rendered passive: 'he was loved'). любили is the past plural of любить; Russian often uses an active past plural without an explicit agent to mean 'they loved him' / 'he was loved' in context.
segments.p2-4-g615: За is the preposition 'for' here (giving the sense 'for what/for what reason'); что is the interrogative pronoun 'what'. Together, За что? is a direct-question phrase meaning 'For what?' or 'What for?'. The leading quotation mark in the original marks the start of direct speech.
segments.p2-4-g616: As in the previous segment, за что is the preposition + interrogative pronoun 'for what'. Here it appears again as the continuation/closing of the quoted question; the closing quotation mark ends the direct speech. The lower-case presentation in the original reflects continuation of the same utterance.
segments.p2-4-g599: И is the coordinating particle 'and' introducing this exclamation. Как бы here is a fixed colloquial/modal phrase ('as it were' / 'almost') formed by как + the modal particle бы; the phrase is used parenthetically to soften or hedge the speaker's feeling.
segments.p2-4-g600: Как бы ... желал combines the same hedging particle (как бы + бы) with the past tense verb желал ('I wished') to express a strong, almost wistful desire. В эту минуту is a temporal adverbial ('at this moment') using в with a time sense; с ними + infinitive помириться ('to reconcile with them') is the construction for wanting to perform a perfective action (помириться) with the prepositional/oblique pronoun ними.
segments.p2-4-g601: Пробило is an impersonal/past use of пробить here meaning 'it struck' (of a clock); восемь часов is the time expression 'eight o'clock' with часов in the genitive as the measure of hours.
segments.p2-4-g602: Наконец is an adverb meaning 'finally' or 'at last', and девять (elliptical here) continues the time: 'nine' — the subject is still the clock (i.e. it became nine / it struck nine).
segments.p2-4-g603: Они is the nominative pronoun 'they' and перешли is the past plural of перейти ('moved / shifted / changed over'), a perfective verb indicating completed movement. Со стола uses the preposition со (variant of с used before certain consonant clusters) with the genitive to mean 'from off the table', and на + accusative диван marks motion onto the sofa.
segments.p2-4-g604: Зверков is a proper name (here the character's name) used as the subject; разлегся is the perfective past reflexive verb meaning 'stretched out / lay back' and на кушетке uses на + locative (prepositional case) to indicate location 'on the couch'.
segments.p2-4-g605: Положив is a gerund (деепричастие) formed from положить and means 'having placed' or 'putting', marking a simultaneous or prior action. Одну ногу (here represented by ногу) is the direct object in accusative 'one leg'; на + accusative столик indicates placement onto the small table, with круглый qualifying 'round'.
segments.p2-4-g689: A direct address using the plural noun господа (vocative/nominal form) meaning 'gentlemen' or 'sirs'; set off by a dash and comma to mark speech. It functions as the addressee of the following utterance.
segments.p2-4-g690: Past tense, masculine singular, perfective verb закричал from закричать meaning 'cried out' or 'shouted'; followed by the speaker's name in nominative as the subject. The comma shows this tag interrupts the direct speech.
segments.p2-4-g691: Деепричастие (adverbial participle) подымаясь ('rising, getting up') expresses an action simultaneous with the main verb; с + genitive (дивана) means 'from the sofa.' Note подымаясь is a variant of подниматься/подыматься used to describe the attendant action.
segments.p2-4-g692: Short spoken command/announcement: теперь ('now') sets the time, всё here means 'everyone' (subject) and туда ('there/over there') gives direction; the main verb (go/come) is omitted and understood, a common colloquial ellipsis in commands.
segments.p2-4-g693: Adverb/interjection конечно meaning 'of course' or 'certainly'; the comma marks the repetition in speech. It functions as an emphatic response.
segments.p2-4-g694: Repetition of конечно for emphasis; exclamatory speech marked by the exclamation point.
segments.p2-4-g695: Past tense plural of заговорить (заговорили) meaning 'began to speak' or 'started talking'; другие is nominative plural 'others' and is the subject. The dash again shows reported speech or an interrupted line of dialogue.
segments.p2-4-g696: Simple past first-person statement: Я 'I' is the subject, круто is an adverb meaning 'sharply/abruptly' modifying the verb, поворотил is past tense 'turned' (here intransitive sense 'turned [one's head/body]'), and к + dative (Зверкову) means 'toward Zverkov.' Word order places the adverb before the verb for emphasis.
segments.p2-4-g711: Ага is an interjection; here it functions as a brief, colloquial exclamation expressing ironic recognition or scepticism rather than a simple affirmative. It stands alone as the entire utterance and carries the speaker's tone (sarcastic/derisive) rather than grammatical relations.
segments.p2-4-g712: This is an elliptic, emphatic clause with the copula omitted (standard in Russian present statements). Дуэль is the topic/nominative noun; the enclitic particle -то after it (дуэль-то) adds contrastive emphasis ('as for a duel' / 'after all a duel...'). Не negates the identification that follows (i.e. it is not the same as). Свой брат is a figurative nominal phrase meaning 'one's own brother' (i.e. a close ally); word order places the topical noun + particle first for forceful contrast.
segments.p2-4-g713: Ядовито is an adverb of manner modifying the verb; it means 'venomously' or 'bittingly'. Прошипел is the past tense, perfective form of 'прошипеть' (a single completed act: 'hissed'); its masculine ending is compatible with the following proper name, which is the nominative subject (Ферфичкин). The verb-before-subject order gives a slightly pointed or narrative tone.
segments.p2-4-g714: This is an impersonal construction: меня (accusative) marks the experiencer ('me'), while резнуло is in the neuter past used with an accusative experiencer to mean 'it stabbed/struck (me)'. Больно indicates that the sensation was painful (an adverb/predicative modifier), and по сердцу is a prepositional phrase giving the location/figurative target ('in/to the heart'). The sentence therefore expresses a sudden, felt emotional pain.
segments.p2-4-g715: «Нет» is an exclamatory negation here meaning simply “No” as a standalone interjection; it precedes the clause to reject or contradict what was just said.
segments.p2-4-g716: Word order places the object (дуэли) before the verb боюсь for emphasis; бояться governs the genitive (дуэли). The verb боюсь is present tense, first person singular, with the negator не before the object and verb to mean “I am not afraid (of the duel).”
segments.p2-4-g717: A direct vocative-like address using the character's name in the nominative; the exclamation mark shows spoken address and emphasis.
segments.p2-4-g718: Я готов is the predicate meaning “I am ready.” с вами is a prepositional phrase with the preposition с + instrumental (вами) meaning “with you.” The particle же here (positioned after вами) adds immediacy/precision—“right/just” — and завтра gives the time “tomorrow.” The infinitive драться completes the predication: “I am ready to fight (physically).”
segments.p2-4-g719: уже here intensifies immediacy and can be translated “already” or “as soon as”; после governs the noun примирения in the genitive to mean “after (the) reconciliation.” Together they indicate the time when the fighting would occur.
segments.p2-4-g720: Я is the subject. даже is an intensifier modifying the verb that follows (“even/indeed”). настаиваю is present tense, first person singular of настаивать “to insist.” The preposition на plus the pronoun этом in the prepositional case form the complement на этом meaning “on this” (i.e. “insist on this point”).
segments.p2-4-g721: и is the coordinating conjunction “and” linking clauses. вы (subject) with не можете (present tense plural of мочь with negation) means “you cannot.” мне is the dative pronoun marking the indirect object (“to me”), and отказать is the infinitive meaning “to refuse/deny,” so the whole clause means “and you cannot refuse me.”
segments.p2-4-g722: Я is the subject and хочу is present tense first person singular of хотеть “to want.” The verb хотят/хочу is followed by the perfective infinitive доказать (“to prove”), and вам is the dative indirect object meaning “to you,” so the phrase means “I want to prove to you.” The use of the perfective infinitive indicates the speaker intends a completed act of proving.
segments.p2-4-g723: что introduces a subordinate clause “that …”. Within it, я is the subject and не боюсь is the negative present tense of бояться (“I am not afraid”), and дуэли is in the genitive because бояться governs genitive, so the clause reads “that I am not afraid of the duel.”
segments.p2-4-g637: "Всеми силами" is an instrumental-case adverbial phrase meaning "with all (one's) strength/might"; it precedes the subject "я" and sets the manner of the action. "я хотел показать" is past tense (хотел) + perfective infinitive (показать) and expresses a past desire/intention: "I wanted to show (demonstrate)".
segments.p2-4-g638: The subordinate clause is introduced by "что" (that). "могу" is present tense of "мочь" (I can / I am able) and governs the infinitive "обойтись"; "и" here is emphatic ("even/also") in the phrase "и без них" meaning "even without them." Together: "that I can manage without them."
segments.p2-4-g639: "А между тем" is a set phrase meaning "meanwhile/in the meantime" ("между" + "тем"). "Нарочно" is an adverb meaning "on purpose/deliberately." "Стучал" is past imperfective (habitual/ongoing past) of "стучать" (to knock/tap/stamp), and "сапогами" is the instrumental plural that names the instrument ("with my boots").
segments.p2-4-g640: "Стано́вясь" is a present adverbial participle (деепричастие) from "становиться," indicating a simultaneous or attendant action: "while/thereby standing / by standing up on..." "на каблуки" is a prepositional phrase meaning "on (one's) heels" (literally "onto heels"), describing how he produced the sound.
segments.p2-4-g641: "Но" is a contrastive conjunction meaning "but/however." "Все" here is the subject meaning "everything/all of it," and "было" is past tense of "to be." "Напрасно" is an adverb meaning "in vain / to no avail." The whole is a simple past statement: "But everything was in vain."
segments.p2-4-g642: "Они-то" places a contrastive emphatic particle "-то" after the pronoun to stress "they (as for them)." "И" here adds an emphatic/colloquial tone ("even/indeed") before the negation. "Не обращали внимания" is past imperfective plural of "обращать внимание" (to pay attention) with "внимания" in the genitive: "did not pay attention / did not even notice."
segments.p2-4-g643: "Я имел терпенье" uses the past verb "имел" (had) with the noun "терпенье" (an older spelling of "терпение", patience); this frames the following infinitive "проходить" (to walk/pass) — i.e. "I had the patience to walk/pass (to go) thus." "Так" is an adverb modifying the manner of walking.
segments.p2-4-g644: "Прямо перед ними" is a spatial adverbial: "прямо" (directly/straight) + preposition "перед" (in front of) governing the pronoun in the instrumental "ними" — together: "directly in front of them."
segments.p2-4-g645: "С ... до ... часов" is a time-range construction: "с" (from) + genitive "восьми" and "до" (until) + genitive "одиннадцати" with "часов" (hours/o'clock) marking the span "from eight to eleven o'clock."
segments.p2-4-g646: "Всё по одному и тому же месту" is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning "always/constantly along the same place/spot." "По" takes the dative "месту;" "одному и тому же" is the fixed collocation "one and the same" ("же" is the emphatic particle).
segments.p2-4-g647: A paired sequence of prepositional phrases indicating movement: "от стола до печки" (from the table to the stove) uses "от" + genitive and "до" + genitive; then "и от печки обратно к столу" repeats "от печки" and adds "обратно" (back) + "к" with dative "столу" (toward/to the table) to show the return.
segments.p2-4-g617: This is a short past-tense first-person statement: думал is the masculine singular past of думать, here meaning 'was thinking/considering' (internal reflection). Я is the subject 'I'. Про + себя is a colloquial reflexive phrase 'to oneself' (literally 'about oneself'), so the whole line is the parenthetical remark 'I thought to myself.'
segments.p2-4-g618: Изредка is an adverb ('occasionally') modifying the past-tense plural verbs. Приходили is imperfective past plural 'would come / would fall into' and here takes the prepositional phrase в пьяный восторг ('into a drunken rapture'); целовались is a past plural reciprocal/reflexive verb meaning 'kissed one another.'
segments.p2-4-g619: Они is the subject 'they'; говорили is past plural imperfective 'talked/spoke' (here 'talked'); о Кавказе is a prepositional-phrase object 'about the Caucasus' (Кавказ in the prepositional case).
segments.p2-4-g620: О том is the fixed prepositional phrase 'about that / about the matter' used to introduce a following clause (о том, что ...). Том here means 'that (matter/thing),' not a physical volume.
segments.p2-4-g621: Что такое functions as an indirect question 'what ... is' (что = 'what', такое = 'such / is like that'), so что такое истинная страсть = 'what true passion is.' Истинная is an adjective 'true,' and страсть is the noun 'passion.'
segments.p2-4-g622: О гальбике is another topical prepositional phrase 'about Galbika' (a proper name given in the prepositional case).
segments.p2-4-g623: О выгодных местах по службе = 'about advantageous positions in the service.' Выгодных (genitive/plural/adjective form) describes места ('places/positions'); по службе is a domain expression 'in the service/officially,' with по indicating context/field.
segments.p2-4-g624: Again о том introduces another reported clause (about the matter of ...).
segments.p2-4-g625: Сколько доходу у гусара Подхаржевского = 'how much income the hussar Podkharzhevsky had.' Сколько ('how much') governs доходу in a partitive/genitive-like sense; у + genitive (у гусара) expresses possession ('the hussar had'). Подхаржевского is the genitive form of the name.
segments.p2-4-g626: Которого is a relative pronoun ('whom/of whom') referring back to the hussar; никто из них не знал лично = 'none of them knew (him) personally.' Знал is past tense 'knew' (to know someone), лично qualifies 'in person.'
segments.p2-4-g627: И радовались is a coordinated past-plural verb 'and (they) rejoiced / were glad,' linking this reaction to the preceding remark about his income.
segments.p2-4-g628: Что у него много доходу is a content clause introduced by 'that' (что): у него = 'he has' (possession construction), много modifies доходу meaning 'a large amount of income.' The word order and case are colloquial/older: много + noun in genitive-like form.
segments.p2-4-g629: О необыкновенной красоте и грации княгини Д‑й = 'about the extraordinary beauty and grace of Princess D‑.' Необыкновенной, красоте and грации form a prepositional-object cluster describing the qualities; княгини is the genitive 'of the princess.' 'Д' is an abbreviated surname/initial (here an abbreviation), the dash/ending is represented by the following element.
segments.p2-4-g630: Которую тоже никто из них никогда не видал = 'whom also none of them had ever seen.' Которую is the feminine relative pronoun (object); видал is a colloquial/earlier past form of 'to see' (instead of видел). Тоже = 'also; too.'
segments.p2-4-g631: Наконец дошло до того is an impersonal phrase 'finally it came to the point' or 'at last it reached the point,' where дошло (neuter singular past of дойти) + до того expresses 'come down to / reach the point that ...'.
segments.p2-4-g632: Что Шекспир бессмертен is the concluding content clause introduced by что: 'that Shakespeare is immortal.' Шекспир is the subject, and бессмертен is a predicate adjective meaning 'immortal' (short form adjective used predicatively).
segments.p2-4-g633: Two past imperfective verbs (улыбался, ходил) describe habitual or repeated past actions; презрительно is an adverb modifying улыбался. The phrase по другую сторону комнаты uses по + accusative to indicate moving/positioning on the other side (a colloquial/idiomatic use of по with accusative).
segments.p2-4-g634: A locative phrase where прямо ('directly/straight') intensifies против + genitive: прямо против дивана = 'directly opposite the sofa.' Против governs the genitive (дивана).
segments.p2-4-g635: Вдоль + genitive (стены) indicates movement lengthwise along the wall. It's a simple adverbial prepositional phrase specifying the path of walking.
segments.p2-4-g636: A paired prepositional construction 'от ... до ...' (both taking genitive) marking extent or motion from one point to another; 'и обратно' adds the return journey. Word order lists the route: from the table to the stove and back.
segments.p2-4-g724: 'Вы будете стрелять первый' uses the compound future: 'будете' (auxiliary 'to be') + infinitive 'стрелять' to make 'you will shoot'. 'Первый' is an ordinal adjective functioning predicatively and agrees with the implied (masculine) person addressed — i.e., 'you will be the first to shoot.' Word order (pronoun + auxiliary + infinitive + predicative adjective) is natural for an emphatic statement of order.
segments.p2-4-g725: 'а' is a contrastive conjunction ('and/but') setting up the speaker's different action. 'Я' is the subject 'I'. 'Выстрелю' is the perfective, simple future 1st person singular of 'выстрелить', signalling a single completed action ('I will fire'). The preposition 'на' with 'воздух' here means 'into/on the air' (i.e. to fire harmlessly into the air) and 'воздух' means 'air'.
segments.p2-4-g726: Сам себя тешит is a short, colloquial clause meaning 'he amuses/consoles himself.' 'Сам' is an emphatic modifier ('himself'/'him personally'), 'себя' is the reflexive object referring back to the subject, and 'тешит' is present tense, 3rd person singular of 'тешить' meaning 'to amuse' here.
segments.p2-4-g727: 'заметил' is the past tense, 3rd person singular of 'заметить' used here as a speech tag ('remarked'). The surname 'Симонов' follows the verb in the typical Russian way of attributing the remark.
segments.p2-4-g728: 'Просто' is an adverb intensifying the exclamation (here rendered 'simply' / 'really'), and 'сбрендил' is a colloquial perfective past-tense verb meaning 'went crazy/has gone mad' (masculine subject implied). The whole is an emphatic one-word judgment.
segments.p2-4-g729: 'Отозвался' is the past tense, 3rd person singular of 'отозваться' used as 'replied' or 'responded' in a reporting clause, and 'Трудолюбов' is the speaker's surname; the verb preceding the name is a normal Russian ordering in such tags.
segments.p2-4-g664: Two comparative adjectives/adverbs ('Бессовестнее' and 'добровольнее') are joined by 'и' and govern the infinitive 'унижать'; the construction is impersonal with 'было невозможно' (it was impossible) following the action. 'себя самому' is a reflexive object with 'самому' adding emphasis (‘to one’s own self’).
segments.p2-4-g665: A short clause-start: 'и я вполне,' places the pronoun 'я' (I) after the conjunction and the adverb 'вполне' (completely) after the subject for emphasis; it prepares the next clause that completes the thought.
segments.p2-4-g666: 'вполне понимал это' is a past imperfective clause meaning 'I fully understood this' ('это' is the direct object). 'и все-таки' is a fixed concessive particle ('nevertheless/still') introducing the action he nevertheless continued: 'продолжал' + infinitive 'ходить' indicates continued/habitual action. 'от стола до печки и обратно' is a standard 'from X to Y and back' adverbial phrase of movement.
segments.p2-4-g667: The single vowel 'О' here is an exclamatory particle equivalent to English 'Oh!' (an interjection).
segments.p2-4-g668: 'если б ... знали' is the conditional/subjunctive ('if only you knew') formed with the particle 'б' after the conjunction 'если' plus the past-tense verb 'знали'; 'вы' is plural/formal 'you' and 'только' is an intensifier meaning 'only/just'.
segments.p2-4-g669: 'на какие чувства и мысли способен я' uses the adjective 'способен' with the preposition 'на' + accusative to mean 'capable of (what) feelings and thoughts'; 'и как я развит' is an exclamatory addition ('and how developed I am'), with 'как' meaning 'how' here.
segments.p2-4-g670: Past imperfective 'думал я' describes a repeated or continued mental action; the instrumental plural 'минутами' indicates duration or periodic intervals ('for minutes'/'for several minutes at a time').
segments.p2-4-g671: Present participle/gerund 'обращаясь' ('addressing/turning to') denotes a simultaneous action: 'мысленно обращаясь к дивану' = 'mentally addressing the sofa'. 'к' governs the dative 'дивану'.
segments.p2-4-g672: 'где' introduces a relative clause giving the place; 'сидели враги мои' is a past plural clause ('my enemies were sitting'), with the possessive 'мои' following the noun for emphasis.
segments.p2-4-g673: Contrastive conjunction 'Но' begins the sentence. 'враги мои' (my enemies) uses postpositive possessive for emphasis; 'вели себя' is the reflexive verb 'вести себя' meaning 'to behave' and 'так' ('so/that way') links to the following comparative clause.
segments.p2-4-g674: 'как будто' introduces a counterfactual/simulative clause ('as if'); the structure 'меня и не было в комнате' is impersonal: 'не было' (there was not) with 'меня' meaning 'me' (i.e. 'as if I were not there'), and the particle 'и' adds emphasis meaning 'not even'.
segments.p2-4-g697: Subject + past-tense auxiliary + short participle: Я (I) is the subject, был is the past tense of быть used here with the short passive participle измучен. The adverbial phrase до того (literally 'to that degree') intensifies the participle: 'was to such a degree exhausted.' The short participle functions like a predicate adjective.
segments.p2-4-g698: Elliptical repetition of the same structure: до того again intensifies, and изломан is another short passive participle/predicate adjective ('broken' or 'broken-down'). The auxiliary был from the previous clause is understood (omitted) here, creating a parallel pair of predicates.
segments.p2-4-g699: Result/degree clause introduced by что ('that'), with хоть functioning as an intensifier meaning 'even'/'to the point of' before the infinitive. The reflexive perfective infinitive зарезаться expresses the extreme action considered (to stab oneself/kill oneself) — here used in an exclamatory/hyperbolic way.
segments.p2-4-g700: А introduces a contrastive or alternative nuance ('and/but'), and покончить is an infinitive meaning 'to put an end to' and here idiomatically implies 'to end (one's life)' (object 'with oneself' omitted). The clause is exclamatory and elliptical: the object of покончить (с собой) is understood.
segments.p2-4-g701: Possessive construction with у + genitive: У меня была = 'I had' (literally 'by me there was'); была agrees in gender with лихорадка (feminine). This is the standard Russian way to express possession in the past.
segments.p2-4-g702: A noun phrase with a participial modifier: смоченные (past passive participle used adjectivally) modifies волосы (subject). Потом here is intended as 'with sweat' (i.e. hair damp from sweat) — the provided sense list did not include that meaning, so it is marked separately. Присохли is past plural of присохнуть ('to dry and stick/adhere'), and ко is the allomorphic form of к used before certain consonant clusters, governing the dative лбу и вискам ('to the forehead and temples').
segments.p2-4-g703: Single-word vocative/exclamation: the dash and the isolated name indicate direct address (calling out to someone). Although the provided lexical senses do not include a proper-name reading, here this token is the surname of the addressee.
segments.p2-4-g704: Direct speech: subject + verb + object with у + genitive marking the source ('ask from you'): я прошу у вас прощенья = 'I ask (from) you forgiveness' (colloquial genitive прощенья for прощение). Word order is neutral but у вас signals 'from you.'
segments.p2-4-g705: Reporting clause: сказал (past tense) with inverted order сказал я (verb before subject for emphasis), modified by the adverbs резко и решительно ('sharply and decisively') which characterize the manner of speaking.
segments.p2-4-g706: A vocative insertion naming the person addressed (Ферфичкин). The dash signals a break in the reported speech and a direct address to that individual.
segments.p2-4-g707: Short addendum: и у вас тоже = 'and you too' with у вас again marking source/target of the apology; тоже adds 'also.' The verb (прошу) is omitted but understood from the earlier clause.
segments.p2-4-g708: u всех = 'from everyone' (literally 'by/of everyone'), repeating the scope of the apology. This is an elliptic phrase where the verb and object remain understood.
segments.p2-4-g709: A repeated, emphatic restatement of the previous phrase (у всех) to intensify the confession. Repetition is rhetorical and common in spoken bursts.
segments.p2-4-g710: Main, emphatic exclamation: я (subject) + past tense obидел ('offended') + всех (direct object, accusative) — straightforward past-tense declarative: 'I have offended everyone!' The short clauses before supply the objects/recipients of the apology.
segments.p2-4-g730: Да here is an optative/exclamatory particle (not a simple 'yes') that softens and urges the request; позвольте is the 2nd-person-plural imperative (polite or addressing a group) of позволить, and пройти is the infinitive functioning as the verb's object/complement (literally 'allow [me] to pass'). The comma marks a sharp shift into the complaint that follows.
segments.p2-4-g731: Что is an exclamatory particle here introducing a reproachful question ('why/how come'); вы is the 2nd-person plural/formal pronoun; поперек дороги is a prepositional phrase meaning 'across the road' with поперёк giving the obstructive sense; стали is the past plural of стать used here in the sense of 'took up position / stood' (literally 'have come to stand'). The whole clause is an indignant question about their blocking the way.
segments.p2-4-g734: Ну is a discourse particle 'well'/'now' introducing the question; чего is the genitive interrogative form of что meaning 'what' (what is needed); вам is the dative pronoun 'to you'; надобно is a predicative adjective/impersonal predicate meaning 'is necessary' — together an idiomatic 'What do you need?' construction with dative + надобно.
segments.p2-4-g735: Презрительно is an adverb modifying the past imperfective отвечал ('replied'); the name Зверков (proper noun) follows the verb, a common stylistic order in Russian narrative. The dash in the original marks that this is a report of speech or reply.
segments.p2-4-g736: Все (placed before the pronoun) means 'all' and emphasizes that every one of the people is meant; они is the nominative plural pronoun 'they'; были is the past-tense plural of 'to be'; красные is a plural nominative adjective used predicatively ('were red' — here likely 'red-faced'). The clause is a simple past predication describing their appearance.
segments.p2-4-g737: Глаза is the nominative plural 'eyes' (subject of блистали). The construction глаза у всех uses у + genitive (всех) to mark possession — 'the eyes of everyone' or 'everyone's eyes'. Блистали is past plural of the imperfective verb 'to shine/glitter', agreeing with глаза.
segments.p2-4-g738: Много is an adverb/modifier meaning 'a lot' or 'much' modifying the verb пили. Пили is past plural of пить ('to drink'), here idiomatically referring to drinking (alcohol). Together they state that they drank a great deal.
segments.p2-4-g675: 'Раз' here is an initial temporal marker meaning 'once' (one single occurrence) used to introduce the event; it functions like an adverbial 'one time' and sets up the singularity of what follows.
segments.p2-4-g676: The phrase 'один только раз' is an emphatic quantifier 'only once'; the clause 'они обернулись ко мне' is a normal past plural verb construction ('они' + past 'обернулись') meaning 'they turned (around) toward me'; note the use of 'ко мне' (ко instead of к) because of phonetic preference before 'м-'.
segments.p2-4-g677: 'именно когда' is a temporal connector meaning 'precisely when' (stresses exact timing). 'Зверков заговорил о Шекспире' is a past action: the proper name is the subject and 'заговорил' (perfective past) means 'began to speak/started talking' about Shakespeare.
segments.p2-4-g678: 'а' here links and contrasts clauses ('and/but'), 'я вдруг' places the sudden action on the speaker, and 'захохотал' is the perfective past 'burst out laughing'; 'презрительно' is an adverb modifying the manner of the laugh ('contemptuously').
segments.p2-4-g679: 'Я так выделанно и гадко фыркнул' describes manner: 'так' intensifies ('so'), 'выделанно' is an adverb meaning 'affectedly/put-on', 'и гадко' adds 'and nastily/disgustingly', and 'фыркнул' is a single past action 'snorted' (derisive human snort).
segments.p2-4-g680: 'что' introduces the result clause ('so... that...'). 'они все разом прервали разговор' means 'they all at once broke off the conversation' ('прервали' past plural perfective). 'молча наблюдали минуты две' uses 'молча' (silently) + imperfective 'наблюдали' to indicate they watched for a continuous period of 'две минуты'.
segments.p2-4-g681: 'серьезно' is an adverb modifying their watching — 'seriously' or 'gravely' — and tightens the manner in which they observed.
segments.p2-4-g682: 'не смеясь' is a negative gerundial phrase ('not laughing'); it modifies the main observing action and means they watched without laughing.
segments.p2-4-g683: 'как я хожу по стенке' uses 'как' meaning 'as'/'how' to introduce the manner in which he moved; 'хожу' is imperfective present/habitual 'I walk/pace' and 'по стенке' (по + dative) expresses movement along the surface of the wall (a colloquial image of pacing along the wall).
segments.p2-4-g684: 'от стола до печки' is a simple path expression 'from the table to the stove'; 'от' + genitive and 'до' + genitive mark the start and end points of the movement.
segments.p2-4-g685: 'и как я не обращаю на них никакого внимания' is another 'как' clause describing manner: 'я не обращаю (на них) никакого внимания' is the idiom 'I pay them no attention' — 'обращаю внимание на' = 'to pay attention to', with 'никакого' reinforcing the negation; 'обращаю' is present imperfective indicating habitual or ongoing behavior.
segments.p2-4-g686: 'Но ничего не вышло' is an impersonal past construction meaning 'but nothing came of it' or 'it turned out to be nothing' — 'ничего' with negated past 'вышло' gives the idiomatic result.
segments.p2-4-g687: 'они не заговорили' means 'they did not start talking' (negative past, they did not strike up conversation), 'и через две минуты' uses 'через' + acc to mean 'after two minutes', and 'опять меня бросили' is colloquial past plural 'they again dropped/abandoned me' — 'бросили' here = 'abandoned/left (me)' rather than literal 'threw'.
segments.p2-4-g688: 'Пробило одиннадцать' is an impersonal idiom meaning 'it struck eleven' (the clock struck eleven); 'пробило' here is the past form of 'пробить' used with time to indicate the hour was struck.
segments.p2-4-g767: Я is the nominative first-person pronoun 'I'. вдруг is an adverb meaning 'suddenly' that modifies the verb. подошел is the masculine singular past tense (perfective) of подойти, meaning a completed action 'approached/went up to'; the perfective aspect implies the action was accomplished. к нему is the preposition к plus the dative pronoun 'him' (нему), giving the direction 'to/toward him.' Word order (subject–adverb–verb–prepositional phrase) is natural and slightly emphatic because the verb precedes the dative phrase.
segments.p2-4-g768: A one-word vocative/exclamatory call using the surname in the nominative (Russian has no distinct vocative case). The dash indicates direct speech; the name is simply being addressed or called aloud.
segments.p2-4-g769: дайте is the 2nd-person plural (or polite) imperative of дать, here used to request or command 'give.' мне is the dative pronoun 'to me' (the indirect object). шесть рублей is the numeral phrase 'six rubles'—note шесть requires the noun in the genitive plural (рублей). The exclamation mark and imperative form mark this as an urgent spoken demand.
segments.p2-4-g770: сказал is the masculine singular past tense (perfective) of сказать, 'said' — here it introduces reported speech. The subject я follows the verb (inverted order), which is common in Russian when the verb is foregrounded. решительно and отчаянно are adverbs modifying how the speaking was done ('decisively' and 'desperately'); и simply links the two adverbs.
segments.p2-4-g755: Олимпия is a proper female name in the nominative used here in direct address; моя is the possessive pronoun 'my' placed after the name for a colloquial or affectionate tone (Russian allows the possessive after the name). The comma marks a brief pause before addressing the group.
segments.p2-4-g756: господа is the plural form used to address a group ('gentlemen') functioning vocatively/appealing to the listeners; the comma indicates direct address. It derives from господин but here simply equals 'gentlemen' or 'sirs'.
segments.p2-4-g757: уговор is a noun used as an exclamation (an elliptical nominal sentence) meaning 'an agreement/a deal' or figuratively 'what a bargain!' — here it functions like an interjection summing up or applauding the proposal.
segments.p2-4-g758: крикнул is the past-tense, perfective masculine form of 'to cry/shout' (he cried/shouted); the verb precedes the name, which is a common word order in Russian narration. Зверков is the speaker's surname in the nominative following the verb; the supplied senses list does not include the proper-name reading, so that sense is not available here.
segments.p2-4-g759: Не is the negative particle placed directly before the verb to negate it; оспариваем is present-tense plural (we) of the imperfective verb 'оспаривать' meaning 'to contest/dispute', so together 'we do not dispute' or 'we're not disputing'. This is a clipped, emphatic reply.
segments.p2-4-g760: Same as the previous segment grammatically: не negates the verb; оспариваем is present plural 'we do not dispute'. The lack of initial dash and the lowercase suggests this is a quick repetition or echo of the same reply rather than a new quoted line.
segments.p2-4-g748: Знайте is the plural/formal imperative of знать meaning 'know' or 'be aware'; here it commands attention from the addressee. It is an emphatic opener that introduces the following content clause.
segments.p2-4-g749: Милостивый государь is a formal, somewhat archaic direct address (nominative used for vocative effect) meaning 'gracious sir' or 'your grace'; the comma marks the pause after the vocative.
segments.p2-4-g750: Что introduces the content clause ('that ...'). Вы is the polite/formal 2nd-person pronoun. Никогда ... ни при каких обстоятельствах is a series of negative/equative particles meaning 'never and in no circumstances' (и here adds emphasis; ни при каких обстоятельствах literally 'at no circumstances' = 'under no circumstances'). Не можете is the negated present of мочь ('cannot/are not able to'), followed by the accusative меня and the infinitive обидеть at the end ('to offend/hurt me'), a typical Russian word order for modal + infinitive.
segments.p2-4-g751: И here functions as a linking/emphatic particle ('and' / marker of abrupt speech). Довольно с вас is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'enough of you' or 'that's enough from you', with с denoting 'from' and вас in the object form.
segments.p2-4-g752: Прочь is an adverb used as an imperative/exclamation meaning 'away!' or 'begone!'; standing alone it functions as a brusque dismissal.
segments.p2-4-g753: Скрепил is the perfective past tense, 3rd person singular masculine of скрепить ('to strengthen/confirm/fasten'); here it means 'affirmed' or 'confirmed' and precedes the name in the same clause, a common narrative order to indicate the speaker's action. Трудолюбов is the speaker's surname in nominative.
segments.p2-4-g754: Едем is the 1st-person plural present of ехать ('we go/are going'); used here as an immediate suggestion or command equivalent to 'let's go' or 'we're off'. The short, standalone utterance is idiomatic for initiating departure.
segments.p2-4-g739: 'Я' is the 1st-person singular subject pronoun. 'Прошу' is the present indicative, 1st person singular of просить (imperfective) meaning 'I ask/request' and takes a direct object in the genitive when asking for something (просить чего). 'Вашей дружбы' is 'your friendship' in the genitive (вашей = feminine genitive singular of ваш; дружбы = genitive singular of дружба). Word order is Subject – Verb – Object; the comma shows the speaker is about to address someone directly.
segments.p2-4-g740: A direct address: 'Зверков,' is vocative/direct-address form (a surname here, set off by a comma). Grammatically it functions as an interjection-like vocative pointing to the person being spoken to; stylistically it may also carry a pejorative tone depending on context.
segments.p2-4-g741: 'Я' repeats the subject for emphasis. 'Вас' is the accusative (formal/plural) object pronoun 'you'. 'Обидел' is the past tense, masculine singular form of обидеть (perfective here) meaning 'offended'/'hurt' — a completed action. Together this clause is a straightforward past-tense apology: 'I offended you.'
segments.p2-4-g742: 'Но' is the coordinating conjunction 'but/however.' Placed alone at the end it leaves the thought unfinished (elliptical), signaling a contrast or a mitigating statement to follow; here it functions pragmatically rather than introducing a full clause.
segments.p2-4-g745: The single-word question 'Обидели?' uses the past-tense plural form of обидеть. In colloquial Russian a past plural can stand in for 'Did you (plural/formal) offend (me)?' or be an elliptical/ironical question 'Offended (us)?' The omitted pronoun makes it a terse, pointed question.
segments.p2-4-g746: This is a stammered form where the initial consonant 'В-' is a broken-off onset and 'вы' is the 2nd-person plural/formal pronoun 'you.' Grammatically nothing else is present; the hyphen marks the stutter. The preposition 'в' is not functioning here as a preposition but as part of the stammer.
segments.p2-4-g747: Another stuttered exclamation: the sequence 'Ми-ня!' represents a split/exclamatory utterance (essentially 'меня' = 'me') with the hyphen showing the stammer. The printed segments (mi, nya) are not standard morphemes here but fragments used to mimic the broken spoken word.
segments.p2-4-g771: This single clause contains the verb поглядел (past, perfective, 3rd person singular) meaning ‘glanced’ or ‘looked briefly,’ followed by a directional/object phrase на меня (на + accusative) indicating who was looked at. The phrase в чрезвычайном изумлении is a prepositional/locative construction (в + noun in prepositional) meaning ‘in/with extraordinary astonishment,’ and the instrumental phrase какими-то тупыми глазами (instrumental plural) describes the manner of looking — literally ‘with some kind of dull eyes,’ where какими-то is the indefinite compound (какими + -то) meaning ‘some/sort of.’
segments.p2-4-g772: Simple predicative sentence: Он (nominative pronoun) is the subject, тоже is an adverb meaning ‘also/too,’ был is the past-tense copula (masculine singular) and пьян is a short-form adjective used predicatively meaning ‘drunk.’ Word order is standard Subject–Adverb–Copula–Predicate.
segments.p2-4-g773: Colloquial interrogative: Да here is an introductory/exclamatory particle, while разве signals incredulity or rhetorical question (‘really? / surely not?’). Вы is the subject (formal/plural you), и is an emphatic/augmentative ‘even/also,’ туда gives direction/place ‘there,’ and с нами (с + instrumental нами) means ‘with us.’ Together they form a surprised question about whether the addressed person will go there with them.
segments.p2-4-g774: Single-word response: Да is an affirmative interjection/particle meaning ‘yes’ or an emphatic assent in speech.
segments.p2-4-g648: Так is an adverb meaning 'that way/so' modifying the verb. Хожу is the imperfective present (first-person singular) of ходить, indicating habitual or repeated action ('I walk/pace'). Себе is the dative/reflexive particle adding a colloquial, self-directed nuance ('to myself' / 'for myself'), so the phrase хожу себе conveys a casual, self-contained habit.
segments.p2-4-g649: И is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. Никто is the negative pronoun 'no one'; it combines with the negative particle не before the verb: никто не может ('no one can'). Может is 3rd-person singular present of мочь ('can/is able to'). Мне is the dative object 'to me', and запретить is an infinitive meaning 'to forbid' — together может мне запретить = 'can forbid me'.
segments.p2-4-g650: Входивший is a past active participle of входить ('who entered') modifying слуга. В комнату uses the accusative with в to indicate motion into the room. Несколько раз is an adverbial phrase 'several times'. Останавливался is the imperfective past (repeated action) 'kept stopping/paused', and the infinitive смотреть expresses purpose/result ('stopped to look') with на меня marking the object of the look.
segments.p2-4-g651: От + genitive (от частых оборотов) expresses cause: 'from/because of the frequent turns'. У меня here is an existential/experiential construction ('at me' → 'I experienced / I had'), and кружилась голова is a normal Russian idiom where голова (subject) takes the verb кружиться in the past to mean 'my head was spinning' / 'I felt dizzy'.
segments.p2-4-g652: Минутами is an adverbial use of the instrumental plural meaning 'at times' or 'for minutes' (indicating intermittence). Мне казалось is the impersonal/dative construction 'it seemed to me' with казалось in the past tense.
segments.p2-4-g653: Что introduces the content clause 'that ...'. Я is the subject pronoun. В бреду is a fixed locative expression meaning 'in delirium' (в + noun form бреду), so я в бреду = 'I was delirious' or 'I was in a delirious state.'
segments.p2-4-g654: В эти три часа uses в + accusative to indicate a span of time ('during these three hours'). Я is the subject. Три раза is an adverbial quantifier 'three times'. Вспотел and просох are perfective past verbs: вспотел ('began to sweat / broke into a sweat') and просох ('dried off'), linked by и ('and') to show successive, completed events.
segments.p2-4-g655: Порой is an adverb meaning 'sometimes'. С introduces an instrumental phrase; глубочайшею is the instrumental feminine/singular form of the superlative 'deepest' (19th-century style ending -ею), here beginning a stacked instrumental modifier that will be completed by 'болью' in the next segment.
segments.p2-4-g656: С ядовитою болью is an instrumental phrase 'with a poisonous pain' (manner/cause). Вонзалась is the past reflexive/inchoative of вонзаться ('pierced / sank in') used figuratively; в мое сердце uses в + accusative to indicate direction ('into my heart'). Мысль is the grammatical subject that is placed after the verb for emphasis ('a thought pierced into my heart').
segments.p2-4-g657: Что introduces the reported content ('that ...'). Пройдет is the perfective future 3rd-person singular of пройти meaning 'will pass' (time will elapse). Десять лет is the time expression 'ten years' serving as the duration that will pass.
segments.p2-4-g658: The phrase continues the enumeration of time spans: 'двадцать лет' = 'twenty years'; here the noun лет is the plural time noun used with numerals.
segments.p2-4-g659: Again an enumerated time span: 'сорок лет' = 'forty years', parallel to the previous items (лет in the plural after a numeral).
segments.p2-4-g660: А is a contrastive conjunction 'and/but'. Я is the subject pronoun 'I'. Все-таки is an emphatic particle meaning 'after all / nevertheless'; here it is written as two tokens (все, таки) for rhetorical pause, but functions together as the concessive/emphatic particle 'still/nevertheless.'
segments.p2-4-g661: Хоть и is a concessive phrase meaning 'even if' or 'although' (хоть + и intensifies the concession). Через сорок лет is a time expression using через + accusative meaning 'in/after forty years' (i.e., even if it is only after forty years).
segments.p2-4-g662: С отвращением и с унижением uses two instrumental phrases 'with disgust and with humiliation' to mark the manner in which the speaker will remember. Вспомню is the perfective future first-person singular 'I will recall / I will remember'. Об + этих introduces the prepositional phrase 'about these ...' that will be completed by the noun 'минутах' below.
segments.p2-4-g663: Смешнейших и ужаснейших are superlative adjectives in the prepositional plural agreeing with минутах ('in these most ridiculous and most terrible minutes'). Минyтах is the prepositional plural of минута used because of the preceding об ('about/regarding these minutes'). Из всей моей жизни is a genitive phrase 'out of/from my whole life' specifying that these minutes are taken from the entirety of his life.
segments.p2-4-g780: A direct vocative call: the dash marks someone calling out. The surname Симонов is in the nominative but functions as an address; no copula or case change is required for direct address in Russian.
segments.p2-4-g781: я видел у вас деньги, — 'я видел' is past tense, masculine singular, literally 'I saw'. The phrase 'у вас' uses the preposition у + genitive to indicate possession ('you had' / 'at your place'), and деньги is accusative plural (identical to nominative) as the direct object of видеть.
segments.p2-4-g782: зачем вы мне отказываете? — 'зачем' asks 'what for/why'; 'вы' is the 2nd-person plural/formal subject; 'мне' is the dative pronoun 'to me' marking the person being refused; 'отказываете' is present tense, 2nd-person plural of отказывать 'to refuse' (transitive with dative). The clause is a direct question about purpose or motive.
segments.p2-4-g783: Разве я подлец? — 'разве' is a particle expressing surprise or incredulity (here 'really? / surely not?'), 'я' is the nominative subject, and 'подлец' is a predicate noun in the nominative (Russian omits the copula in the present tense). The question expects denial or disbelief.
segments.p2-4-g784: Берегитесь мне отказать: — 'берегитесь' is the 2nd-person plural/reflexive imperative of беречься meaning 'beware' or colloquially 'don't you dare'; it is followed by the infinitive отказать 'to refuse'. The dative 'мне' marks the person who would be refused, so the whole means 'beware of refusing me' or 'don't you dare refuse me'.
segments.p2-4-g785: если б вы знали, — 'если' is 'if'; the particle 'б' (short for бы) combines with the past tense to form a counterfactual/optative ('if only'); 'вы' is the subject, and 'знали' is the past tense plural of знать, giving the sense 'if you knew' or more idiomatically 'if only you knew'.
segments.p2-4-g786: если б вы знали, — this is a repeat of the previous clause for emphasis; same conditional past construction ('if only you knew'), reinforcing the speaker's appeal and expectation that the addressee lacks crucial knowledge.
segments.p2-4-g787: для чего я прошу! — 'для' governs a genitive and here combines with 'чего' (colloquial genitive of что) to mean 'for what' or 'for what purpose'; 'я' is the subject and 'прошу' is 1st-person singular present of просить 'I ask/request'. As an exclamation it means 'what I'm asking for!' or 'what I'm begging for!'.
segments.p2-4-g775: У меня (literally 'at me') is the standard Russian possession construction meaning 'I have'; денег is the genitive plural of деньги used after нет to express absence, and нет is the existential negation 'there is no'—together: 'there is no money at me' → 'I don't have any money'. The exclamation mark shows this is an emphatic spoken reply.
segments.p2-4-g776: Отрезал is past tense, masculine singular of отрезать; here it is used figuratively to report a curt, cutting verbal reply ('snapped'/'cut him off'), and the verb placed before он gives a succinct narrative tone. Он is the nominative pronoun 'he' referring back to the speaker of the preceding line.
segments.p2-4-g777: Презрительно is an adverb modifying усмехнулся; усмехнулся is a perfective past verb meaning 'smirked' or 'gave a contemptuous little smile'. И connects the two past actions, and пошел (past of пойти) means 'set off / went' (here: left), with из + genitive (комнаты) indicating motion 'out of the room.'
segments.p2-4-g778: Я (subject) + схватил (perfective past 'grabbed/seized') indicates a completed, physical action; его is the direct object 'him', and за + accusative (шинель) here expresses grabbing 'by' or 'at' the coat (taking hold of the garment). The word order is straightforward subject–verb–object, with за specifying how/where he was seized.
segments.p2-4-g779: Это был is the past-tense copular construction ('it was' / 'this was'); кошмар is the predicate noun meaning 'nightmare' used figuratively to describe a dreadful situation. The sentence is a concise evaluative comment on the preceding action.
segments.p2-4-g788: 'От этого' is the preposition от + genitive demonstrative pronoun (этого) meaning 'from/on account of/depending on this'; 'зависит' is 3rd person singular present indicative of зависеть 'to depend (on)'; 'все' is a pronoun here meaning 'everything' and is the grammatical subject that depends on 'этого'. Word order (prepositional phrase + verb + subject) is normal for emphasis on the cause.
segments.p2-4-g789: все мое будущее is a noun phrase: 'все' (adjectival/quantifying) + possessive pronoun 'мое' + noun 'будущее' (neuter). Here 'все' functions like 'whole' or 'entire' modifying 'будущее' ('my whole future'), with normal adjective + possessive + noun order.
segments.p2-4-g790: все мои планы is another noun phrase parallel to the previous one: 'все' (here 'all') + 'мои' (possessive plural) + 'планы' (plural noun) — 'all my plans'. The repetition of все links these phrases back to the verb 'зависит' in the previous segment.
segments.p2-4-g791: Симонов is the subject (proper name). 'вынул' is past tense, perfective, 3rd person singular of вынуть 'to take out/produce (from pocket)'; 'деньги' is the direct object (plural). The coordinate clause 'и чуть не бросил их мне' follows: 'чуть не' + past verb expresses 'almost did'. 'бросил' is past of бросить 'to throw', 'их' is the object pronoun referring to 'деньги', and 'мне' is the dative indirect object 'to me' (he almost threw them to me).
segments.p2-4-g792: Возьмите is the polite/plural imperative (perfective) of взять 'to take' (addressing вы). It is a direct command: 'Take (it).'
segments.p2-4-g793: если introduces a conditional clause 'if'; 'вы' is the 2nd-person plural/formal subject; 'так' here modifies the adjective meaning 'so/that much'; 'бессовестны' is the short-form plural adjective (present) agreeing with вы, meaning 'shameless/unscrupulous'. The whole clause means 'if you are so shameless.'
segments.p2-4-g794: безжалостно is an adverb 'ruthlessly/mercilessly' modifying the verb; проговорил is past tense, perfective, 3rd person singular of проговорить 'to utter/say' (here 'said/uttered'); the postverbal 'он' is a neutral subject pronoun placed after the verb for a slightly emphatic or stylistic word order; 'и' links the two actions; 'побежал' is past tense of побежать 'to start running' (perfective) meaning 'ran off'; 'догонять' here is an infinitive 'to catch up with' used with побежал to indicate purpose (ran in order to catch up); 'их' is the pronoun 'them' (accusative).
segments.p2-4-g761: Отвечали is past tense, plural (imperfective) of отвечать — 'answered' or 'were answering'. Ему is the dative pronoun 'to him' showing the indirect object. Смеясь is a деепричастие (adverbial participle/gerund) from смеяться, 'laughing' — it expresses the manner of the answering and is linked to the main verb rather than having its own subject.
segments.p2-4-g762: Я is the first-person pronoun 'I'. Стоял is past tense, masculine singular of стоять ('stood/was standing'). Оплеванный is a past passive participle used predicatively (like an adjective) meaning 'spat on' or 'covered with spit' — it describes the subject's state at the time he was standing.
segments.p2-4-g763: Ватага is a noun meaning a crowd/rabble and serves as the subject. Шумно is an adverb 'noisily'. Выходила is the past tense, imperfective, feminine/third-person singular form of 'to go out/leave' (describing the ongoing action of leaving). Из + genitive комнаты marks motion out of the room; the word order places the adverb between subject and verb for emphasis.
segments.p2-4-g764: Трудолюбов is a proper name (surname) in the nominative as subject. Затянул is past singular masculine of затянуть; with a noun for a song it means 'launched into/began to sing' (a verb of beginning or carrying on the singing). Какую-то is the indefinite expression formed by какой + the particle то, here meaning 'some' — какую (interrogative/relative form) plus то (a particle that makes it indefinite). Глупую песню is the feminine accusative object 'a silly/stupid song' governed by затянул.
segments.p2-4-g765: Симонов is a proper name in the nominative (subject). Остался is past tense, perfective or resultant-stative sense of 'to remain/stay' (he stayed or remained). На + accusative минутку expresses duration ('for a minute' — here 'for a tiny moment'), with крошечную as the accusative feminine adjective modifying минутку.
segments.p2-4-g766: Чтоб is a purpose conjunction 'in order that / so as to' and here governs the infinitive дать 'to give'. На чай is a fixed phrase where на + accusative indicates the purpose/use of the money or gift ('for tea'). Слугам is dative plural 'to the servants', the indirect object receiving what is given.
segments.p2-4-g808: 'Или' at the start is a rhetorical 'or' introducing one of two possible (contrasting) actions; word order is neutral. 'они все' = 'they all' (pronoun + quantifier). 'на коленах' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'on their knees' (на + locative plural), placed after the subject to indicate posture.
segments.p2-4-g809: 'обнимая' is a present active participle (деепричастие) meaning 'embracing' or 'clasping' and describes a simultaneous action to the main verb; 'ноги мои' is the direct object phrase with the possessive after the noun (poetic/emphatic order) = 'my legs'.
segments.p2-4-g810: 'будут вымаливать' is the analytic future tense (plural) formed with the auxiliary 'будут' + imperfective infinitive 'вымаливать', conveying a repeated or prolonged action ('will be begging persistently'). 'моей дружбы' is a genitive of relation ('for my friendship' / 'my friendship' as the object of begging).
segments.p2-4-g811: This final 'или.' is a trailing, rhetorical 'or' left hanging to mark an alternative left unsaid; the period and isolation emphasize the unfinished contrast.
segments.p2-4-g814: Again 'или' is the rhetorical 'or' setting up an alternative. 'я дам' is 1st person singular future perfective ('I will give' / here idiomatically 'I will slap'). 'Зверкову' is dative, marking the person who receives the action, and 'пощечину' is the accusative singular object ('a slap'). The whole clause is abrupt and exclamatory.
segments.p2-4-g815: 'Тише' is the comparative/adverbial form used as an interjection meaning 'hush' or 'be quieter'; the asterisk likely marks a stage direction or abrupt command to fall silent.
segments.p2-4-g816: The parenthetical '(франц.)' is an abbreviation indicating language or origin — here 'French' — often used in older texts to mark a phrase as from French; it's not a full Russian grammatical phrase but a note.
segments.p2-4-g795: A simple past-tense clause with subject-verb-object/time phrase. 'Я' is the nominative subject 'I'. 'остался' is the perfective past form of остаться meaning 'remained' or 'stayed' (masculine singular), and the time expression 'на минуту' follows the verb to indicate duration ('for a minute').
segments.p2-4-g796: A nominative noun used as the first item in a series of nouns describing the scene; no verb is required because this is a paratactic list (cataloguing what is present).
segments.p2-4-g797: Another nominative plural noun in the list; the comma shows continuation of the enumerative series describing leftovers/food scraps.
segments.p2-4-g798: 'разбитая' is a past participle/ adjective (feminine nominative) agreeing with the feminine noun 'рюмка'. The locative phrase 'на полу' (preposition + locative) follows to mark the glass's position 'on the floor.' Together this forms a single noun phrase in the nominative as another list item.
segments.p2-4-g799: 'пролитое' is a past passive participle used attributively before the neuter noun 'вино' (they agree in gender/number), forming another nominative noun phrase 'spilled wine.'
segments.p2-4-g800: A noun phrase where 'окурки' (nominative plural) is modified by the following noun in the genitive 'папирос' to indicate type/possession: 'butts of papiros' (i.e., cigarette butts).
segments.p2-4-g801: Two nouns joined by 'и' ('and'): 'хмель' here means drunkenness/intoxication, and 'бред' means delirium; the locative 'в голове' (in the head/mind) applies to them and specifies where these states are felt.
segments.p2-4-g802: 'мучительная' (adjective) modifies 'тоска' (nominative), forming 'agonizing melancholy/longing'; the prepositional phrase 'в сердце' indicates location in the emotional sense ('in the heart'); the conjunction 'и' connects this item to the final one introduced next.
segments.p2-4-g803: An adverb meaning 'finally' or 'at last,' used to introduce the concluding element of the list.
segments.p2-4-g804: A nominative noun 'лакей' ('the footman' or 'lackey') — the head noun that the following participial clause will modify. It is the final item in the enumerative series.
segments.p2-4-g805: A chain of past active participles ('видевший', 'слышавший', 'заглядывавший') modifying 'лакей' and describing his repeated/past actions: 'having seen everything and having heard everything and inquisitively peering.' 'все' (before видевший and слышавший) means 'everything/all'; 'любопытно' is an adverb 'curiously/inquisitively;' 'мне' is dative 'at me/to me'; the preposition 'в' with plural accusative 'глаза' expresses direction 'into the eyes.' The participles are attributive and form a single descriptive clause attached to 'лакей.'
segments.p2-4-g806: A single adverb 'Туда' used as an exclamation or directive meaning 'to there' / 'that way'; no explicit verb is needed in this elliptical utterance.
segments.p2-4-g807: 'вскрикнул' is the perfective past masculine singular of вскрикнуть ('to cry out, to shriek'), placed before the pronoun in inverted word order; 'я' is the nominative subject. The clause is the speech tag reporting who made the exclamation.
